title: Mmm1 progress note temp filling
content:
Based on the NDEB guidelines discussed in the lecture transcript, here are the corresponding scenario descriptions and model progress notes for the amalgam filling and the temporary filling.

### Scenario 1: Amalgam Restoration (New Patient Consultation)
**Clinical Scenario:**
A new patient presents for an initial examination and is diagnosed with dental caries on the mesial and occlusal surfaces of the maxillary right first molar (Tooth 1.6). Since this is the first time meeting the patient, a full medical history is obtained. You discuss the diagnosis and recommend a mesio-occlusal (MO) amalgam restoration. You discuss the risks, benefits, alternatives (such as a composite restoration), and the associated fees. The patient consents to the treatment plan and is booked for the procedure next week.

**Model Progress Note:**

**Date:** [Insert Date]  
**NDEB ID:** [Insert ID]

**Subjective**
Medical history obtained; reviewed systems with the patient. No medical conditions identified, and the patient confirms they are not taking any medications.

**Objective**
Clinical and radiographic examination completed. Caries identified on tooth 1.6 MO. 

**Treatment / Procedure**
*   **Diagnosis & Plan:** Diagnosis of dental caries on 1.6 MO. The recommended treatment discussed with the patient is an amalgam restoration. 
*   **Consent:** Informed consent obtained for the 1.6 MO amalgam restoration. Discussed the nature of the treatment (including the use of local anesthesia, rubber dam isolation, and the placement of a metal-colored filling). 
*   **Alternatives & Fees:** Discussed advantages, disadvantages, risks, and alternatives (composite restoration). Treatment fees and costs were discussed with the patient. The patient confirmed understanding and provided consent to proceed.

**Next Appointment**  
Patient booked at the front desk for a 1.6 MO amalgam restoration next week.

**NDEB ID:** [Insert ID]

***

### Scenario 2: Temporary Filling (Following an Unplanned Pulpotomy)
**Clinical Scenario:**
A returning patient is scheduled for a deep filling. Medical history is reviewed and remains unchanged. During the planned restorative procedure under local anesthesia and rubber dam isolation, deep caries removal leads to an unexpected carious pulpal exposure. To manage the exposure, you perform an emergency full pulpotomy. Because a definitive root canal treatment cannot be completed today, you place a temporary filling (e.g., IRM or Glass Ionomer) to seal the tooth and relieve symptoms.[1]

**Model Progress Note:**

**Date:** [Insert Date]  
**NDEB ID:** [Insert ID]

**Subjective**
Medical history reviewed; patient confirmed no changes. Patient medications confirmed to be up to date.

**Objective**
Palpation: Negative. 
Percussion: Negative. 
Radiographs taken and reported: Deep caries identified approximating the pulp. 

**Treatment / Procedure**
*   **Consent:** Informed consent reviewed with the patient for the restorative procedure. *Note: Once the pulp was exposed, the patient was informed mid-procedure about the change in treatment plan to a pulpotomy.*
*   **Anesthesia:** Local anesthesia given: 36 mg of 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine administered via infiltration. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Local anesthesia confirmed subjectively and objectively.
*   **Isolation & Preparation:** Rubber dam applied and clamped. A high-speed handpiece was used for caries removal. Deep caries removal resulted in a carious pulpal exposure. 
*   **Pulpotomy & Temporization:** Hemostasis was achieved. An emergency full pulpotomy was performed to manage the vital, cariously exposed pulp. Due to the inability to complete definitive endodontic treatment today, a temporary filling was placed to provide a coronal seal. Rubber dam removed. Occlusion checked and adjusted.[1]

**Post-Operative Instructions & Communication**
The patient was informed about the depth of the decay, the resulting pulpal exposure, and the emergency pulpotomy performed. The patient was explicitly advised that the filling placed today is temporary and that definitive endodontic (root canal) treatment and a final permanent restoration are required in the future. Advised to avoid sticky, chewy, or hard foods on the side of the temporary restoration to prevent fracture or dislodgement. Instructed to report if severe pain or swelling develops. The patient understood all instructions and was dismissed.

**Next Appointment**  
To be booked at the front desk for a definitive endodontic evaluation and continued treatment.

**NDEB ID:** [Insert ID]

Sources
[1] Pulp Therapy for Primary and Immature Permanent Teeth https://www.aapd.org/media/Policies_Guidelines/BP_PulpTherapy.pdf
[2] NDEB Endodontic Exam Practice Questions | PDF | Dental Anatomy https://www.scribd.com/document/697292888/6-released-2015-english
[3] NDECC Blueprint Details Released | NDEB Update - YouTube https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yRcOw7RcrRo
[4] First case class 2 Amalgam Any opinions to improve my skills https://www.facebook.com/groups/586101082020576/posts/1365113540785989/
[5] 2019 NDEB - Comprehensive Exam Review Questions and Answers https://www.studocu.com/row/document/university-of-kabianga/nursing-skills/2019-ndeb-comprehensive-exam-review-questions-and-answers/138109031
[6] Class II Amalgam Restoration - My Dental Key https://mydentalkey.com/lesson/class-ii-amalgam-restoration/
[7] NDEB (CANADIAN) EXAM WITH 100% CORRECT ANSWERS 2026 https://www.docsity.com/en/docs/ndeb-canadian-exam-with-100percent-correct-answers-2026/16179434/
[8] LSU Health Pediatric Dentistry https://lsupedsdent.com/note-templates
[9] All About the NDEB Equivalency Process - iGotIn https://www.igotin.com/advice/all-about-the-ndeb-equivalency-process
[10] [PDF] Guideline on Pulp Therapy for Primary and Immature Permanent Teeth https://www.aapd.org/assets/1/7/G_Pulp.pdf

title: Mmm1 progress note selective caries removal
content:
Based on the lecture transcript provided, the speaker is discussing the National Dental Examining Board of Canada (NDEB) standards for clinical documentation. Here is the reconstructed clinical scenario and the corresponding model progress note.

## Clinical Scenario
A returning patient presents to the clinic for a scheduled restorative appointment. During a previous visit, the patient was diagnosed with deep caries on the maxillary right first molar, and a Mesio-Occluso-Distal (MOD) composite restoration was treatment planned. 

Today, the patient's medical history is reviewed with no changes. The patient reports no spontaneous pain, and clinical examination reveals negative responses to both percussion and palpation. Radiographs confirm a deep carious lesion closely approximating the pulp. During the procedure, the decay is found to be extremely deep. To avoid pulpal exposure and maintain pulp vitality, you decide to perform selective caries tissue removal (an indirect pulp cap). You successfully place the composite restoration.[1][2]

## Model Progress Note

**Date:** [Insert Date]  
**NDEB ID:** [Insert ID]

**Subjective**
Medical history reviewed; patient confirmed no changes. Patient medications confirmed to be up to date.

**Objective**
Palpation: Negative. 
Percussion: Negative. 
Radiographs taken and reported: Deep caries identified on tooth 1.6 approximating the pulp. 

**Treatment / Procedure**
*   **Consent:** Informed consent reviewed with the patient for a 1.6 MOD composite restoration. Note: The NDEB uses the FDI numbering system, and formatting it as "1.6" prevents confusion with the Universal numbering system.[3]
*   **Anesthesia:** Local anesthesia given: 36 mg of 2% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine administered via buccal and palatal infiltration. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Local anesthesia onset was confirmed subjectively by the patient and objectively via explorer probe.
*   **Isolation & Preparation:** Rubber dam applied and clamped on tooth 1.7 using clamp #13. A high-speed handpiece was used for decay removal. Decay was removed to the base of the cavity. Due to the depth of the lesion, selective caries tissue removal was performed at the base to prevent pulpal exposure. The cavity was cleaned thoroughly.
*   **Restoration:** Calcium hydroxide was placed at the base of the cavity. A matrix band and wedges were applied. A 3-step etch and rinse system was utilized. Composite resin was placed and shade matched.
*   **Finishing:** The matrix band, wedges, and rubber dam were removed. Interproximal contacts, occlusion, and aesthetics were checked and verified. 

**Post-Operative Instructions & Communication**
The patient was informed about the depth of the caries, its proximity to the pulp, and the selective caries tissue removal approach taken. The patient was advised of the future possibility of requiring endodontic treatment if symptoms develop, and was instructed to report any pain. The patient was informed that mild sensitivity is common and should wear off in 2 to 3 weeks. The patient was advised to avoid sticky and chewy foods on the right side for 24 hours. Warned that gums may be temporarily sore due to the rubber dam clamp, and warm salt water rinses were recommended for comfort. The patient understood all instructions and was dismissed satisfied with the procedure.

**Next Appointment**  
To be booked at the front desk for recall. 

**NDEB ID:** [Insert ID]

Would you like me to generate the progress note scenarios for the amalgam and temporary fillings that the speaker mentioned at the end of the transcript?

Sources
[1] Selective Caries Removal: A Case Report with 21-Year Follow-Up https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11260509/
[2] Network dentists use selective caries removal for deep carious lesions https://www.nationaldentalpbrn.org/blog/network-dentists-use-selective-caries-removal-for-deep-carious-lesions/
[3] Enhancing Precision: Proposed Revision of FDI's 2-Digit Dental ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10988244/
[4] [PDF] Dental Caries https://www.cda-adc.ca/en/services/essentials/2016/issue8/files/assets/common/downloads/publication.pdf
[5] [PDF] Selective Caries Removal in Permanent Teeth (SCRiPT) for the ... https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/176217/1/Selective%20Caries%20Removal%20in%20Permanent%20Teeth%20(SCRiPT)%20for%20the%20treatment%20of%20deep%20carious%20lesions%20a%20randomised%20controlled%20clini.pdf
[6] Selective caries removal and restoration https://www.childcaries.sdcep.org.uk/guidance/dental-techniques/selective-caries-removal/
[7] Selective caries removal – take it or leave it? - Solventum https://www.solventum.com/en-us/home/oral-care/brain-floss/blog/2022/12/selective-caries-removal-take-it-or-leave-it/
[8] Dental notation systems | Anatomy.app https://anatomy.app/encyclopedia/dental-notation-systems
[9] Teeth Numbering System | PDF | Dentistry | Dental Anatomy - Scribd https://www.scribd.com/document/836591924/Teeth-numbering-system
[10] The International Tooth Numbering System (FDI) - Pressbooks.pub https://pressbooks.pub/oralsurgery4rns/part/the-international-tooth-numbering-system/

title: Mmm1 progress note x2
content:
Here are two scenarios based on the typical variations tested in the NDECC Situational Judgement exam, along with their respective model progress notes formatted perfectly as a continuous SOAP paragraph. 

### Scenario 1: New Patient Consultation (Treatment Planning Only)
**Scenario Details:**
A 45-year-old new patient, Sarah Smith, presents to your clinic for a specific problem exam. She complains of food getting stuck between her lower right back teeth, causing mild discomfort when chewing. 
- **Medical History:** Positive for controlled hypertension (takes Amlodipine 5mg daily). No allergies. 
- **Examination:** A large carious lesion is found on the mesio-occlusal (MO) surface of tooth 46. No signs of pulpal involvement; cold test is normal.
- **Radiograph:** One periapical and one bitewing taken. Caries on 46 MO extends into the middle third of dentin. Normal bone levels, no periapical radiolucency. 
- **Task:** Write a progress note for this consultation appointment where you diagnosed the issue and obtained consent for a future amalgam restoration. You did not perform the filling today.[1]

**Model Answer 1:**
[Doctor ID: 12345] [Date: March 12, 2026] Medical history obtained and reviewed with the patient; positive for hypertension, currently controlled with Amlodipine 5mg daily, no known allergies reported. Patient Sarah Smith, 45 years old, attended the clinic unaccompanied with a chief complaint of food impaction and mild chewing discomfort in the lower right posterior region. Extraoral examination was within normal limits. Intraoral examination revealed a large cavitated carious lesion on the mesio-occlusal surface of tooth 4.6. Palpation and percussion tests on 4.6 were normal, and cold vitality testing elicited a normal, non-lingering response. Two radiographs (one periapical, one right bitewing) were taken and reviewed; the radiographic report shows caries on the mesial aspect of 4.6 extending into the middle third of the dentin, with no pulpal exposure, normal bone levels, and no periapical radiolucency observed. The diagnosis for tooth 4.6 is a mesio-occlusal carious lesion with a normal pulp and normal apical tissues. The prognosis for the tooth is excellent with restorative treatment. Discussed the diagnosis and proposed treatment plan of a 4.6 MO amalgam restoration with the patient. Informed consent was obtained after discussing the nature of the treatment, including the need for local anesthesia and a rubber dam, as well as the advantages, disadvantages, alternative options (such as composite resin), and associated risks. Fees and costs were also discussed, and the patient agreed to proceed with the amalgam restoration. Patient was booked at the front desk for the restorative procedure next week. [Doctor ID: 12345]

***

### Scenario 2: Procedural Complication (Broken File during Endodontics)
**Scenario Details:**
Patient Mark Jones, 30 years old, returns to your clinic for a previously planned root canal treatment on tooth 24 due to irreversible pulpitis. 
- **Medical History:** Reviewed, no changes since last week. Healthy, no meds.
- **Procedure:** During the cleaning and shaping of the palatal canal of tooth 24, a rotary NiTi file separated (broke) in the apical third. You attempted to bypass/retrieve it but were unsuccessful. You placed a temporary medication, sealed the tooth, informed the patient, and referred him to an endodontist.[2]
- **Task:** Write the progress note documenting the procedure, the complication, the patient communication, and the referral.

**Model Answer 2:**
[Doctor ID: 12345] [Date: March 12, 2026] Medical history reviewed with patient Mark Jones, 30 years old, who attended unaccompanied; patient confirmed no changes to his medical history, remains healthy with no medications or allergies. Informed consent for the root canal treatment on tooth 2.4 was reviewed prior to the procedure, with emphasis on the potential risks, including the specific risk of instrument separation. Administered 1 cartridge (1.8 mL) of 2% Lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine via buccal and palatal infiltration; local anesthesia was confirmed to be profound and the patient tolerated the injection well. A rubber dam was placed and clamped on tooth 2.5 for isolation. Access cavity was prepared on tooth 2.4 and two canals (buccal and palatal) were located. During the biomechanical preparation of the palatal canal using rotary nickel-titanium instruments, a file separated in the apical third of the canal. Attempts to bypass and retrieve the separated instrument using ultrasonic instruments and hand files were unsuccessful. The canals were copiously irrigated with sodium hypochlorite, dried with paper points, and calcium hydroxide was placed as an intracanal medicament. The access cavity was sealed with a temporary restorative material and the rubber dam was removed. The patient was immediately informed about the separated instrument in the palatal canal, the unsuccessful retrieval attempts, and how this complication may impact the overall prognosis of the tooth. A referral to an endodontic specialist was strongly recommended for the management of the separated file and the completion of the root canal treatment. Post-operative instructions were given, advising the patient to avoid chewing on the left side to prevent fracture of the temporary filling and to take over-the-counter analgesics as needed for mild soreness. The patient understood the situation, agreed to the specialist referral, and was dismissed in stable condition; a referral letter to the endodontist was provided to the patient at the front desk. [Doctor ID: 12345]

### Key Takeaways for the NDECC Exam:
- **Format:** Both notes are written as a single, continuous paragraph with no line breaks, which is a strict legal requirement for NDECC progress notes to prevent "tampering" with records.
- **Consultation vs. Procedure:** Notice how Scenario 1 explicitly states "Medical history obtained" and includes fee discussions, while Scenario 2 states "Medical history reviewed" and skips fee discussions because it was a booked procedural appointment.
- **Complications:** If a complication occurs (like a broken file), you must document that you informed the patient of the incident, how it impacts prognosis, and what the subsequent plan is (referral).

Sources
[1] Common mistakes in the NDECC® Situational Judgement Exam ... https://www.facebook.com/prepdoctors/posts/common-mistakes-in-the-ndecc-situational-judgement-exam-can-cost-you-an-entire-s/808898124993614/
[2] A Study of Adverse Events Associated with Endodontic Files ... https://athenaeumpub.com/a-study-of-adverse-events-associated-with-endodontic-files-utilizing-the-manufacturer-and-user-facility-device-experience-maude-database-2/
[3] Master the NDECC® Situational Judgement Exam step ... - Facebook https://www.facebook.com/prepdoctors/posts/master-the-ndecc-situational-judgement-exam-step-by-stepthe-situational-judgemen/881531867730239/
[4] Preparing for SJTs? We're here to help you! Our video will guide you ... https://www.facebook.com/thebritishdentalassociation/posts/preparing-for-sjts-were-here-to-help-you-our-video-will-guide-you-through-the-ex/1234699485360571/
[5] NDECC Situational Judgement - Acecourses https://acecourses.ca/courses/situational-judgement
[6] How to pass NDECC Situaional Judgement Exam- Answering most asked questions on Facebook forums https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kZZnf7DzQV0
[7] Stunning AI‑Powered SOAP Notes Examples | DentScribe https://www.dentscribe.ai/ai-soap-notes-examples
[8] We are looking for case studies to share their quit smoking stories, to ... https://www.facebook.com/groups/1646310865629805/posts/3776935602567310/
[9] The broken file to retrieve or to leave ? - Style Italiano Endodontics https://endodontics.styleitaliano.org/the-broken-file-to-retrieve-or-to-leave/
[10] Class I Amalgam Preparation & Restoration | Operative Dentistry https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0rAMAqZVRnc

title: Mmm1 progress note trauma uncomplicated
content:
Here is a complete scenario and a model progress note formatted to meet the requirements of the NDECC Situational Judgement exam, focusing on the SOAP (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan) format.[1][2]

### Scenario Details

**Patient Profile:** 
A 28-year-old patient, John Doe, presents to the clinic with a chief complaint of a chipped upper right front tooth (tooth 11) that occurred yesterday while eating. He states it is slightly sensitive to cold but not painful. 

**Clinical Findings:**
- **Medical History:** No significant medical conditions, no known allergies, not taking any medications.
- **Examination:** Tooth 11 has an uncomplicated crown fracture involving the mesial-incisal edge (enamel and dentin). No pulpal exposure. Percussion and palpation are normal. Cold test is positive but lingers normally (reversible). 
- **Radiograph:** One periapical radiograph of tooth 11 was taken. It shows a fracture extending into the dentin, no pulpal involvement, and no periapical radiolucency. 
- **Procedure Performed:** Direct composite restoration on tooth 11 under local anesthesia. 

**Candidate Task:**
Write a comprehensive progress note using the SOAP format detailing the visit, the procedure, and post-operative instructions, ensuring all legal and NDECC-specific requirements are met.

***

### Model Progress Note Answer

**[Date: March 12, 2026]**
**[Doctor ID: 12345]**

**Subjective:** 
Patient John Doe, 28 years old, attended the clinic unaccompanied. Medical history reviewed: no significant medical conditions, no known allergies, and no current medications. The patient's chief complaint is a chipped upper right front tooth (tooth 11) that broke yesterday while eating. The patient reports mild sensitivity to cold but denies any spontaneous pain or pain upon biting. 

**Objective:** 
Extraoral examination is within normal limits. Intraoral examination reveals an uncomplicated crown fracture on the mesio-incisal aspect of tooth 11, exposing dentin but without visible pulpal exposure. Palpation and percussion tests on tooth 11 are normal. Cold vitality testing on tooth 11 elicited a normal, non-lingering response. 
Radiographic Report: One periapical radiograph of tooth 11 taken. Radiograph shows a mesio-incisal fracture extending into the dentin, well away from the pulp horn. Bone levels are normal, and no periapical radiolucency is observed.

**Assessment:** 
Diagnosis for tooth 11 is an uncomplicated crown fracture with normal apical tissues and a normal pulp. The prognosis for the tooth is excellent. 

**Plan / Procedure:** 
Discussed treatment options, risks, and benefits with the patient. Patient consented to a direct composite restoration on tooth 11. 
Procedure: Administered 1 cartridge (1.8 mL) of 2% Lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine via local infiltration. Tooth 11 was isolated and prepared. The tooth was etched, bonded, and restored using shade A2 composite resin. The restoration was contoured, polished, and occlusion was checked and adjusted. The patient tolerated the procedure well. 
Post-operative instructions given: The patient was advised to avoid biting directly on hard foods with the front teeth to prevent the new restoration from chipping or dislodging. Advised that mild sensitivity to cold is normal for a few days but to return if pain worsens. 
Next appointment: Patient scheduled for a standard recall exam and cleaning in 6 months.

**[Doctor ID: 12345]**

***

### Key Takeaways for the NDECC Exam:
- **No Bullet Points:** The NDECC explicitly requires progress notes to be written in a single, continuous paragraph without spaces or bullet points. Bullets are only allowed for other written stations (like treatment options).
- **Radiograph Rule:** If you mention taking a radiograph in the "Objective" section, you *must* provide a radiograph report detailing what you saw (even if it is just confirming normal anatomy) to avoid a critical error.
- **Post-Operative Instructions:** Write out "post-operative instructions given" fully—do not use acronyms like "POIG." Furthermore, specify exactly what instructions were given to protect yourself legally.
- **Sign-off:** Always open and close the note with your 5-digit NDECC ID, not your name. If you make a mistake, strike a single line through the error, write the correction, and initial it using your NDECC ID in brackets.

Sources
[1] How to write SOAP notes (examples & best practices) - SimplePractice https://www.simplepractice.com/resource/how-to-write-soap-notes/
[2] Best Practices for Creating and Keeping Dental Records - Berxi https://www.berxi.com/resources/articles/best-dental-record-practices/
[3] Master the NDECC® Situational Judgement Exam step ... - Facebook https://www.facebook.com/prepdoctors/videos/master-the-ndecc-situational-judgement-exam-step-by-step-the-situational-judgeme/2390310658038876/
[4] How to Write Nursing Progress Notes: A Practical Guide - Imploy https://www.imploy.com.au/healthcare/resources/write-nursing-progress-notes
[5] Medical SOAP Note Example and Best Practices: A Guide to Writing ... https://studyingnurse.com/writing-guides/medical-soap-note-example/
[6] PROGRESS NOTE TYPE 1. Date http://www.publichealth.lacounty.gov/sapc/NetworkProviders/ClinicalForms/AQI/ProgressNoteTemplateSOAP.pdf
[7] [PDF] Direct composite restoration of 11 and 21 https://bacd.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/direct-composite-restoration-of-11-and-21.pdf
[8] Nursing Test Bank https://www.naxlex.com/nursing/question/a-nurse-is-using-soap-format-to-document-a-clientrsquos-progress-note-
[9] Students' performance in clinical class II composite restorations - PMC https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11536952/
[10] [PDF] Dental Records: Best Practices for Information Management and ... https://assets.ctfassets.net/u2qv1tdtdbbu/7xGNrpsLZ2K1DTboFiv8T2/ea7bb4b68421be4eedf1c6143d5509e9/ce532.pdf

title: Mmm1 failing rct need retreat
content:
Here is the complete scenario and a model answer script based on the situation, structured specifically to meet the communication and patient-centered care rubrics for the NDECC Situational Judgement (SJ) exam.[1]

### Scenario Details

**Patient Profile:** 
A patient presents to your clinic with a complaint of a bad, foul taste in their mouth and a "pimple" (gum boil) on their gums above an upper front tooth. The patient notes that they are not currently in any pain. 

**Clinical & Radiographic Findings:**
- **History:** The affected anterior tooth had a root canal treatment in the past by a different provider.
- **Radiograph:** Shows a failing/reinfected root canal, a broken endodontic instrument left inside the canal from the previous procedure, and a chronic apical abscess (radiolucency at the apex).

**Candidate Task:**
Within 10 minutes, gather any missing context, explain the clinical and radiographic findings to the patient in layman’s terms, deliver a formal dental diagnosis, and propose a treatment recommendation without disparaging the previous dentist.[1]

***

### Model Answer Script

**1. Standard Opening & Verification**
*(Look directly at the simulator/patient)*
"Hi, my NDECC ID is [Insert your 5-digit ID]." 
*(Pause for 5-10 seconds and wait for the simulator to repeat your ID)*

**2. Information Gathering & Open-Ended History**
"Hi there, thank you for coming in today. Before we discuss the findings from your exam and x-rays, I wanted to ask about the previous root canal on this front tooth. Were you advised of any specific follow-ups for this tooth, or was anything specific shared with you by the previous dentist at the end of that procedure?"
*(Listen to the patient's response. If they do not know about the instrument, do not act shocked or accuse the previous dentist).*

**3. Explaining the Findings (Layman's Terms)**
"Thank you for sharing that. Let's go over what I found today. Inside your tooth, where the nerve used to be, there is a small piece of a dental instrument that was left behind during the previous procedure. This can sometimes happen during complex root canals. 

Because of this, it appears bacteria have found their way back inside the tooth, causing a new infection. That 'pimple' or swelling you're seeing on your gums is actually the infection trying to escape. The reason you are having that foul taste—and the reason you aren't feeling any pain right now—is because the infection is constantly draining out into your mouth instead of building up pressure inside the bone."

**4. Delivering the Official Dental Diagnosis**
"However, this infection will not heal on its own and the tooth needs help. In dental terminology, the official diagnosis for what you are experiencing is a **reinfected root canal with a chronic apical abscess**."

**5. Treatment Recommendation & Value of the Tooth**
"Because this is a very important front tooth, we absolutely want to save it, and it has a good prognosis if we act now. To clear this infection, the inside of the tooth needs to be completely re-cleaned and the old root canal needs to be redone. 

Because this is a complex situation involving the previous treatment and the separated instrument, my recommendation is to refer you to a dental specialist called an Endodontist. They have the advanced micro-equipment needed to handle this safely and give your tooth the best chance of survival."

**6. Continuity of Care & Closure**
"The specialist will only be taking care of the root canal itself. Once they have cleared the infection, you will come right back to me, and I will be the one to place the final crown on the tooth to protect it permanently. I will remain your primary dentist for everything else. 

I know this is a lot of information to take in. Do you have any questions for me, and do I have your consent to proceed with writing that referral letter to the specialist?"

***

### Key Takeaways for the NDECC Exam:
- **Tone:** Ensure your tone remains calm, empathetic, and objective. 
- **Professionalism:** Never say, "The last dentist messed up." State the presence of the instrument neutrally as a factual finding.[1]
- **Pacing:** Hit the exact required diagnosis clearly, as missing the diagnosis or failing to offer the specialist referral is considered a critical error on this station.

Sources
[1] How to pass NDECC Situaional Judgement Exam- Answering most asked questions on Facebook forums https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kZZnf7DzQV0
[2] Role play scripts for NDECC exam preparation - Facebook https://www.facebook.com/groups/252454122423054/posts/1415304236138031/

title: Non verbal patient SDM
content:
This is a reorganized revision guide for the **NDECC Situational Judgement (SJ)** exam. The content has been structured into logical steps, focusing on **Clinical Protocol**, **Ethical Reasoning**, and **Critical Errors** to avoid.

---

# NDECC SJ Scenario: Non-Verbal Patient & Consent

**Scenario:** A non-verbal patient arrives for an extraction (invasive procedure) accompanied by their son.

### 1. The Core Ethical Principle (The "Theme")

- **Non-Verbal $\neq$ Lack of Mental Capacity.**
- A patient’s inability to speak does not mean they cannot understand, appreciate consequences, or make decisions.
- **Goal:** Maximize patient autonomy. You must make every reasonable effort to communicate directly with the patient before looking to a third party.

---

### 2. Critical Errors (Fatal Flaws)

- 🚫 **Assuming Incompetence:** Automatically assuming the patient lacks mental capacity just because they cannot speak.
- 🚫 **Bypassing the Patient:** Speaking immediately to the son for consent without attempting to engage the patient.
- 🚫 **Assuming the SDM:** If the patient *does* lack capacity, blindly assuming the son is the correct Substitute Decision Maker (SDM) without verifying the legal hierarchy.

---

### 3. Step-by-Step Management Protocol

### Step 1: Establish Communication (The "How")

Do not rush. Modify your interaction style to suit the patient.

- **Direct Engagement:** Maintain eye contact with the patient, not the son.
- **Pace:** Speak slowly and clearly (the patient is non-verbal, not necessarily deaf; they can likely hear you).
- **Tools:**
    - Use pen and paper.
    - Use a tablet/digital device for typing.
    - Observe non-verbal cues (nodding, thumbs up/down, eye gaze).
    - Watch for negative cues (confusion, blank stare, dazed look).

### Step 2: Assess Mental Capacity (The "What")

You must determine if the patient understands the information and appreciates the consequences of the extraction.

- *Question:* "Do you understand why we need to remove this tooth?"
- *Observation:* Does the patient write a coherent answer? Do they nod appropriately?

### Step 3: The Decision Tree (If/Else)

**Scenario A: Patient HAS Mental Capacity**

- **Action:** Proceed with the Informed Consent discussion directly with the patient.
- **Role of Son:** He is a support person only. He has **no** decision-making authority.
- **Process:** Explain risks, benefits, and alternatives using simple language and visual aids. Confirm understanding via writing or gestures.

**Scenario B: Patient LACKS Mental Capacity**

- *Criteria:* Patient is unresponsive, dazed, or clearly does not understand despite all communication efforts.
- **Action:** You must now identify the appropriate **Substitute Decision Maker (SDM)**.
- **SDM Hierarchy (Ontario/RCDSO Standard):**
    1. Guardian (Court appointed)
    2. Attorney for Personal Care
    3. Representative appointed by Consent & Capacity Board
    4. Spouse or Partner
    5. **Child (The Son)** or Parent
    6. Sibling
- **Process:** Verify the son is the highest-ranking available person. If confirmed, conduct the informed consent discussion with the son.

---

### 4. Documentation (Record Keeping)

Your notes are your legal defense. For a non-verbal patient, documentation must be detailed.

1. **Patient Status:** Note that the patient is "non-verbal."
2. **Modifications:** Record the steps taken to communicate (e.g., "Used iPad to type questions," "Patient provided written responses," "Spoke slowly and used diagrams").
3. **Capacity Assessment:**
    - *If capable:* "Patient demonstrated understanding via [method] and provided consent."
    - *If incapable:* "Patient was unable to appreciate consequences due to [observation, e.g., lack of response/confusion]. Deemed to lack capacity for this specific treatment."
4. **SDM Verification:** If consent was taken from the son, note that you verified his status as the appropriate SDM according to the hierarchy.

---

### 5. Key Rationale Phrases for Your Answer

Use these phrases to demonstrate professional judgment:

- "My professional obligation is to facilitate communication and not rob the patient of their autonomy."
- "I will not conflate a communication barrier with a cognitive deficit."
- "Informed consent is a process, not just a signature. I must ensure the patient comprehends the risks of this invasive procedure."
- "I must adhere to the HCCA (Health Care Consent Act) hierarchy to identify the correct Substitute Decision Maker."

### Summary Checklist

- [ ]  Did I talk to the patient first?
- [ ]  Did I use visual aids/writing tools?
- [ ]  Did I assess capacity separately from speech?
- [ ]  Did I check the hierarchy before letting the son sign?
- [ ]  Is it all written in the chart?

Sources

title: 51 61 avulsion
content:
To assist with your NDECC Situational Judgement (SJ) exam revision, I have organized the transcript into a structured clinical protocol. This format highlights the critical errors to avoid and the key communication points required to pass the station.

### **Station Scenario: Avulsion of Primary Incisors**

- **Patient:** 4-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Traumatic avulsion of 51 and 61 (primary maxillary central incisors).
- **Presentation:** Parent brought the teeth wrapped in a handkerchief.
- **Parent's Concern:** Anxious about the missing teeth, asking about putting them back, and requesting a space maintainer/denture because they are worried about space loss, eating, and speech.

---

### **1. Critical Errors (Automatic Fail)**

Avoid these actions at all costs:

- **Replanting the teeth:** Never replant primary teeth. This risks damaging the permanent tooth bud (which is developing directly above the primary root).
- **Fabricating a space maintainer/denture:** Do not agree to make a removable appliance for a 4-year-old in this scenario. It poses an aspiration risk and is clinically unnecessary.
- **Dismissing the parent:** Do not simply say "no" without providing a rationale. You must explain *why* treatment is not indicated.

---

### **2. Clinical Management Strategy**

### **Step 1: Introduction & Reassurance**

- **Acknowledge the situation:** "I see you’ve brought your daughter in immediately following the fall. You did the right thing by coming straight here."
- **Address the "Handkerchief":** Acknowledge they brought the teeth.
    - *Script:* "Thank you for bringing the teeth with you. It shows you were trying to do your best for your daughter."
- **Immediate Clinical Decision:** State clearly that the teeth will not be put back.
    - *Script:* "After examining the teeth and your daughter, I can confirm that these are baby teeth. In this specific situation, we do not put them back in."

### **Step 2: Rationale for Non-Replantation (The "Why")**

- **Protecting the Permanent Successors:**
    - *Script:* "Directly underneath these baby teeth, the adult teeth are currently developing. If we try to push these baby teeth back in, we risk damaging the permanent teeth, which could cause them to come in crooked, discolored, or malformed. Our priority is protecting those adult teeth."
- **Next Steps:** Mention taking a radiograph (X-ray) to check for root fragments and ensure the permanent buds are safe.

### **Step 3: Addressing the Request for a Space Maintainer**

The parent will likely ask: *"But won't her teeth shift? How will she eat? Does she need a spacer?"*

- **Address Space Loss (Key Concept):**
    - **Primary Anterior Teeth:** Unlike posterior teeth, the anterior segment is stable at age 4.
    - *Script:* "That is a great question. Fortunately, at 4 years old, the jaw has grown enough that these spaces will not close up. Baby front teeth do not drift into empty spaces like adult teeth do. The space will remain open naturally until the adult teeth come in around age 6 or 7."
- **Address Function (Eating/Speech):**
    - *Script:* "She will still be able to eat comfortably using her back teeth. You may want to cut her food into smaller pieces for a few days, but she will adapt very quickly. Her speech development is also advanced enough that this will not cause any long-term issues."
- **Address Safety (Why no denture?):**
    - *Script:* "We generally avoid removable plates or false teeth for children this young because they present a choking hazard. If a small piece were to break off or if the appliance became loose, she could swallow or inhale it. The safest option is to leave the area to heal naturally."

### **Step 4: Post-Op Instructions & Education**

- **Home Care:** Soft diet, keep the area clean.
- **Future Education (The "Teachable Moment"):**
    - *Script:* "Just for future reference—and I hope this never happens again—if an **adult** tooth is ever knocked out, that is a true emergency where we *do* want to put it back. If you can't put it back, transport it in **milk** (not a dry handkerchief or water) and rush to us immediately. But for today, with these baby teeth, you don't need to worry."

---

### **3. Key Competencies Being Tested**

1. **Diagnosis:** Recognizing 51/61 are primary teeth and knowing the protocol (do not replant).
2. **Communication:** De-escalating a worried parent and validating their effort (bringing the teeth) even though the treatment (replanting) is wrong.
3. **Knowledge Application:** Explaining *why* space maintainers are contraindicated in the primary anterior dentition (aspiration risk vs. minimal space loss risk).
4. **Health Promotion:** Educating the parent on how to handle a permanent tooth avulsion (store in milk) for the future.

Sources

title: Extraction 26 progress note
content:
This is a comprehensive organization of the transcript provided, structured specifically for **NDECC Situational Judgement (SJ)** revision.

The transcript focuses on a **Record Keeping** scenario involving a **Complicated Extraction**.

---

# NDECC Study Guide: Surgical Extraction with Complication (Crown Fracture)

## 1. Case Scenario Snapshot

- **Patient:** Adult female, scheduled appointment.
- **Tooth:** 26 (Maxillary Left First Molar).
- **Diagnosis:** Non-restorable, Necrotic Pulp, Asymptomatic Apical Periodontitis.
- **Radiograph:** Large carious lesion, extensive structural loss, periapical radiolucency, roots fully formed, **close proximity to maxillary sinus**.
- **The Event (Complication):** During extraction, the clinical crown fractures.
- **Outcome:** You successfully remove all roots (surgical or elevator).
- **Task:** Write a complete Progress Note.

---

## 2. The Golden Rules for This Station

The transcript emphasizes specific "must-haves" to pass this station:

1. **Event Management:** You *must* clearly document that the fracture happened and *exactly* how you fixed it.
2. **Informed Consent:** Must clearly state that **fracture** and **surgical extraction** were discussed as risks *before* starting.
3. **Sinus Precautions:** Because the image showed sinus proximity, the note must mention checking for sinus communication.
4. **Confirmation:** You must document that the socket was inspected and no root tips remain.
5. **Specific Vitals:** Never write "vitals stable." Write the numbers (e.g., BP 120/80, HR 72).

---

## 3. The Model Progress Note (SOAP Format)

Here is the reconstructed note based on the "Ideal Rubric" described in the text.

### **Subjective (History)**

- **Reason for Visit:** Patient presented for scheduled extraction of tooth 26.
- **Medical History:** Reviewed and updated. No changes reported (NKDA, no contraindications).
- **Current Status:** Patient asymptomatic at time of appointment.
- **Pre-op Vitals:** BP: 120/80 mmHg, HR: 72 bpm.

### **Objective (Findings)**

- **Clinical:** Tooth 26 shows extensive structural loss; non-restorable.
- **Radiographic:** Pre-operative radiograph reviewed. Confirmed periapical radiolucency consistent with pathology. Roots fully formed. Close proximity to maxillary sinus noted. No acute swelling or sinus tract present.

### **Assessment (Diagnosis)**

- **Diagnosis:** Tooth 26: Necrotic Pulp with Asymptomatic Apical Periodontitis.
- **Restorability:** Deemed non-restorable.

### **Plan & Procedure (The Critical Section)**

- **Informed Consent:** Verbal and written consent obtained.
    - *Specific Risks Discussed:* Pain, swelling, infection, bleeding, **root fracture**, **sinus communication**, and **possible need for surgical extraction/bone removal**. Patient understood and agreed.
- **Anesthesia:** 2 cartridges (3.6ml) 2% Lidocaine w/ 1:100k Epinephrine via buccal infiltration and palatal injection. Patient tolerated well.
- **Procedure Start:** Soft tissue reflected. Forceps application.
- **The Event:** **During luxation, the clinical crown of 26 fractured.**
- **Management of Event:**
    - Remaining root structure visualized.
    - *(Option A - Elevation):* Roots carefully separated and elevated individually.
    - *(Option B - Flap):* Full thickness mucoperiosteal flap raised (mesial relieving incision). Buccal bone guttered under copious saline irrigation.
- **Confirmation:** All roots/fragments successfully removed.
    - **Socket Inspected:** No retained root fragments.
    - **Sinus Check:** **No sinus communication noted** (Valsalva or visual inspection).
    - **Debridement:** Socket curetted to remove granulation tissue; irrigated with normal saline.
- **Closure:** Hemostasis achieved with gauze pressure. (If flap raised: *3-0 Silk sutures placed, x2*).

### **Dismissal (Post-Op)**

- **Instructions:** Verbal and written post-op instructions given.
    - *Specifics:* Bite on gauze, soft diet, avoid straws/spitting/smoking.
    - *Red Flags:* Contact office if prolonged bleeding, fever, or swelling worsens after day 3.
- **Plan:** Return in 1 week for suture removal (if applicable) or healing check.
- **Dismissal Status:** Hemostasis confirmed. Patient stable and ambulatory.
- **Post-op Vitals:** BP: 122/82 mmHg, HR: 76 bpm.
- **Sign-off:** Dr. [Your Candidate ID]

---

## 4. Grading Checklist (The "Crux")

Use this checklist to review your practice notes for this scenario. If you miss these, you lose points.

| Component | Requirement | Check |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Vitals** | Did you write specific numbers (Pre AND Post)? | [ ] |
| **Radiograph** | Did you mention reviewing it + the sinus proximity? | [ ] |
| **Consent** | Did you document discussing the specific risk of *fracture* or *surgery*? | [ ] |
| **The Event** | Did you clearly write "Crown fractured"? | [ ] |
| **Management** | Did you explain *how* you got the roots out (flap/bone removal/elevation)? | [ ] |
| **Safety** | Did you mention "Copious irrigation" during bone cutting? | [ ] |
| **Clearance** | Did you write "No root tips remaining" / "Socket inspected"? | [ ] |
| **Sinus** | Did you explicitly rule out sinus communication? | [ ] |
| **Pathology** | Did you mention curettage/removal of granulation tissue? | [ ] |
| **ID** | Did you sign with your Candidate ID? | [ ] |

Sources

title: Replace AF filling
content:
This is a classic NDECC Situational Judgement station involving **Patient Management, Evidence-Based Dentistry, and Ethics (Non-maleficence).**

Here is the transcript organized into a structured revision guide, broken down by **Phases of the Conversation**, **Key Arguments**, and **Sample Verbiage**.

---

# NDECC SJ Revision Guide: Request to Replace Sound Amalgam Fillings

### 1. The Scenario

- **Patient Profile:** Has 3 sound (serviceable) amalgam restorations.
- **The Request:** Wants them changed to composite (tooth-colored) fillings.
- **The Motivation:** Read online that metal fillings cause Multiple Sclerosis (MS), are toxic, or are banned in other countries.
- **Clinical Reality:** No breakdown, no caries. Removal is medically unnecessary and potentially harmful.

### 2. Core Ethical Principles

- **Non-maleficence (Do No Harm):** Removing sound fillings releases mercury vapor unnecessarily and removes healthy tooth structure.
- **Beneficence:** acting in the patient's best interest (keeping a durable restoration).
- **Patient Autonomy vs. Professional Integrity:** You respect their wishes, but you cannot perform treatment that is not indicated or is potentially harmful based on evidence.

---

### 3. Step-by-Step Response Strategy

### Phase 1: Information Gathering (The "Why")

**Do not** start with a refusal or a lecture. You must understand their specific concern first.

- **Goal:** Build rapport and identify the source of misinformation.
- **Script:** *"I understand you want to replace these fillings. Before we discuss that, may I ask what is the specific reason behind this request?"* or *"I am curious to know what prompted this decision?"*

### Phase 2: Validate and Validate (Don't Argue)

**Do not** attack their source (Instagram/Facebook). Acknowledge their effort to care for their health.

- **Goal:** Lower defenses so they will listen to your education.
- **Script:** *"I appreciate you sharing that with me. It is fantastic that you are taking an active role in researching your dental health. There is a lot of information out there, and it can be overwhelming."*

### Phase 3: Evidence-Based Education (The "Pivot")

Explain how dentists evaluate information (Critical Appraisal) without sounding arrogant.

- **Concept:** The Hierarchy of Evidence (Randomized Controlled Trials > Expert Opinion/Social Media).
- **Key Fact:** Major health organizations (CDA, ADA, FDA) confirm amalgam is safe.
- **Script:** *"In healthcare, we follow a strict protocol called 'Critical Appraisal.' We look at the highest level of evidence—like systematic reviews and randomized control trials. Based on the most current scientific data from the Canadian Dental Association and other top authorities, there is no evidence linking amalgam fillings to Multiple Sclerosis or toxicity."*

### Phase 4: Risk Assessment (The "Refusal")

Explain *why* you are hesitant to do it (Harm > Benefit).

- **Risk 1:** **Transient Mercury Release.** Removing the filling generates heat and vapor, exposing them to the very thing they fear (mercury) more than leaving it alone.
- **Risk 2:** **Structural Integrity.** Changing from amalgam to composite often requires removing more tooth structure.
- **Risk 3:** **Longevity.** Composite generally has a shorter lifespan and higher risk of recurrent caries compared to amalgam.
- **Script:** *"My primary goal is your safety. Paradoxically, removing these stable fillings can cause a transient release of mercury vapor during the drilling process, which exposes you to unnecessary risk. Furthermore, switching to composite might weaken the tooth or lead to recurrent decay sooner. Leaving them alone is actually the safest option for you right now."*

### Phase 5: Addressing the "Ban" Argument

If they ask: *"Why are other countries banning it?"*

- **The Fact:** The **Minamata Convention** focuses on **environmental protection**, not patient safety.
- **Explanation:** It is about reducing mercury waste in the ecosystem (water/fish), not because the filling in the tooth is toxic to the human.
- **Script:** *"That is a great question. The restrictions in some countries are actually based on environmental conservation efforts to reduce mercury waste in our water systems, not because the fillings are unsafe for patients' health."*

---

### 4. Critical "Do's and Don'ts"

| **DO** | **DON'T** |
| --- | --- |
| **Do** ask "Why?" immediately. | **Don't** say "No, I won't do it" in the first sentence. |
| **Do** validate their research efforts. | **Don't** ridicule their source (e.g., "Don't trust Facebook"). |
| **Do** cite the CDA/ADA/regulatory bodies. | **Don't** cite personal opinion ("I think it's fine"). |
| **Do** focus on "Harm vs. Benefit." | **Don't** cave in if they pressure you (maintain professional integrity). |
| **Do** mention "Transient Vapor Release." | **Don't** guarantee composite will last as long as amalgam. |

### 5. Conclusion / Exit Strategy

- **Ideally:** The patient understands and agrees to monitor the fillings.
- **If Patient Insists:** Hold your ground politely. Do not perform treatment you believe is harmful.
- **Last Resort:** *"If you feel strongly that this must be done despite my recommendation, I may have to refer you to a colleague, as I cannot ethically perform a procedure that I believe will cause you more harm than benefit."* (Note: Only use this if the patient is relentless; usually, the station ends before this).

Sources

title: SLE patient on steroid
content:
| Detail | Information |
| --- | --- |
| Patient age | 68 years old (geriatric) |
| Medical history | Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) — autoimmune disorder |
| Medication | Prednisone 10 mg/day × 15 years (= ~40 mg hydrocortisone equivalent chronically) |
| Chief complaint | Grossly carious, non-restorable tooth (broken down to gum line) |
| Station type | Communication/PCC — treatment options + modifications |

---

## Station Structure: How to Open

Always open with the **NID (Name–Introduction–Date of birth)** and immediately **acknowledge the prompt information** — do not re-ask what was already given.[1]

> *"I understand you have been diagnosed with SLE 15 years ago, and you are currently taking Prednisone 10 mg daily for this condition, under the care of your physician."*
> 

Then **signpost your two main chunks** clearly:

1. Treatment options for the tooth
2. Possible modifications to that treatment, given your medical history

> *"I have two main things to discuss with you today — first, the tooth and your options, and second, any adjustments we may need to make given your health background. Does that sound okay?"*
> 

---

## Part 1: Treatment Options

### Why the Tooth Is Non-Restorable

Briefly explain **before** listing options (30 seconds max):

- Extensive decay has broken the tooth down to the gum line
- All walls are lost — no structure remains to support a restoration
- Therefore, saving the tooth is not clinically feasible

### Option 1 — Extraction ✅ *(Most Recommended)*

- Removes the source of infection
- Straightforward recommendation for a non-restorable tooth
- Acknowledge functional impact (chewing/mastication) — replacement options (implant, bridge, denture) can be discussed at a later appointment once healing is complete

### Option 2 — No Treatment ❌ *(Least Recommended — must still be listed)*

The exam requires **plural options** — you cannot list only one. Present no-treatment last, lead with consequences, then loop back to Option 1.[1]

**Consequences of no treatment:**

- Infection can spread (cellulitis, abscess, systemic spread)
- More bone loss over time
- Future treatment becomes more complex, expensive, and invasive
- In an immunocompromised patient (SLE), infection risk is elevated[2][3]

> *"While you do have the option to leave the tooth alone, I strongly advise against it — here is why… [consequences]. Given all of this, I recommend we move forward with the extraction."*
> 

---

## Part 2: Treatment Modifications

### Step 1 — Assess Anxiety First

Before discussing modifications, ask specifically about anxiety using a structured scale (e.g., Modified Dental Anxiety Scale — 5 questions, scored 1–5 each):

> *"How are you feeling about today's appointment? On a scale of 1–5, how anxious would you say you are right now?"*
> 

**Why this matters:** Anxiety + chronic corticosteroid use → worsens adrenal suppression → risk of adrenal crisis.  This is a key red flag.[4]

In this case: patient scores fours and fives → **highly anxious** → red flag confirmed.

---

### Red Flags for Adrenal Insufficiency Risk

The **standard guideline** is: only patients with **primary adrenal insufficiency** require steroid supplementation.  Secondary/tertiary suppression (from exogenous corticosteroids like prednisone) does **not** routinely require supplementation  — **EXCEPT** when risk factors are present:[5][6][7][4]

| Red Flag | This Patient |
| --- | --- |
| **Geriatric age** | ✅ 68 years old |
| **High anxiety** | ✅ Scores 4–5/5 on anxiety scale |
| **Poorly controlled/chronic infection** | ✅ Grossly carious, broken-down tooth |
| **Possible procedure conversion** | ✅ Simple extraction may convert to surgical (flap + bone removal) |
| **Long-term exogenous corticosteroid use** | ✅ Prednisone 10 mg × 15 years |
| **No prior surgical history to reference** | ✅ First dental surgical procedure |

> All four major red flags are present → **pause and refer to physician before proceeding.**[4]
> 

---

### Modifications to Discuss with the Patient

### 🔴 Physician Consult (Primary Modification)

Explain *why* you cannot extract today, and seek **patient consent** for the consult:

> *"Before we proceed with the extraction, for your safety, I would like to contact your physician. I want to make sure your body is prepared to handle this procedure, given your medical history."*
> 

**Ask the physician three things:**

1. **Corticosteroid supplementation** — Is a steroid bump needed given the patient's age, high anxiety, chronic use, and risk of surgical conversion?[8][4]
2. **Antibiotic prophylaxis** — Routine prophylaxis is NOT required per AHA guidelines for SLE alone, but given the combination of autoimmune disease + long-term high-dose corticosteroids + immunosuppression, AHA itself recommends physician consultation for this subgroup[9][10]
3. **CBC (Complete Blood Count)** — Specifically platelet count; if platelets < 50,000, significant bleeding risk exists[3][2]

### 🟡 CBC / Blood Work (Secondary — Yellow Flag)

- Check latest CBC: focus on **platelet count** and **WBC** (leukopenia risk in SLE/corticosteroid patients)[10]
- If not recent, request one before proceeding

### 🟢 Aspirin / NSAID Awareness

- SLE patients may be on aspirin — confirm with patient/physician[3]
- NSAIDs post-operatively: **not contraindicated**, but **limit dose and duration** due to aspirin interaction and bleeding risk[3]

### 🟢 Anxiety Management (Do Not Overlook!)

Mentioning anxiety management shows true **patient-centered care (PCC)**:

- Morning/short appointment[3]
- Calm, unhurried environment
- Nitrous oxide (N₂O) sedation consideration
- Music/distraction
- Frequent check-ins during procedure
- **Post-operative blood pressure monitoring for up to 8 hours** (evidence-based recommendation)[4]

---

## Closing the Station

1. Summarize the plan clearly
2. Explain why you are pausing today (patient safety, not avoidance)
3. Seek **explicit consent** for physician consult
4. Confirm rebooking once clearance is received

> *"So today, rather than proceeding with the extraction, I would like your consent to reach out to your physician. Once I hear back, I will book you in as soon as possible to get that tooth removed. Does that make sense? Do you have any questions for me?"*
> 

---

## Quick-Ref Rubric Checklist

| Rubric Component | ✅ Covered? |
| --- | --- |
| NID + acknowledge prompt | Must do |
| Signpost two chunks (options + modifications) | Must do |
| Non-restorability explained | Must do |
| Option 1: Extraction — most recommended | Must do |
| Option 2: No treatment + consequences | Must do |
| Anxiety assessed (MDA scale) | Must do |
| Red flags for adrenal insufficiency identified | Must do |
| Standard guideline stated (no routine supp. for 2° AI) | Must do |
| Red flags justify physician consult | Must do |
| Consult: steroid supplementation question | Must do |
| Consult: antibiotic prophylaxis question | Must do |
| CBC / platelet count mentioned | Should do |
| NSAID caution mentioned | Should do |
| Anxiety management plan (N₂O, short appt, BP monitoring) | Should do |
| Patient consent for consult obtained | Must do |
| Natural, organized, patient-centered tone throughout | Must do |

---

## Key Phrases to Remember

- *"There is a **possibility** that supplementation **may** be required…"* — never say "definitely"[4]
- *"Your physician knows you best — they will decide."*
- *"This is for your safety — I want to make sure we are fully prepared before we proceed."*
- *"The evidence supports that routine supplementation is not required for patients on daily steroids — however, in your specific case, given your age, anxiety, and the nature of this procedure, there are factors that change that picture."*[6][4]

Sources
[1] OSCE: DESIGN, DEVELOPMENT AND DEPLOYMENT - PMC https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6398515/
[2] Systemic lupus erythematosus: a review for dental ... https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9680889/
[3] Dental Management of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (A Case ... https://www.scirp.org/journal/paperinformation?paperid=142456
[4] FACT SHEET: Adrenal Insufficiency (also known as “AI” ... https://cdho.org/factsheets/adrenal-insufficiency/
[5] What steroid supplementation is required for a patient with primary ...pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov › ... https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24930256/
[6] Corticosteroid Supplementation - Dimensions of Dental Hygiene https://dimensionsofdentalhygiene.com/article/corticosteroid-supplementation/
[7] Steroid supplementation required if primary adrenal ... https://primarycarenotebook.com/pages/diabetes-and-endocrinology/steroid-supplementation-required-if-primary-adrenal-insufficiency-and-undergoing-a-dental-procedure

title: Aspirin post extraction scenario
content:
---

## The Clinical Scenario

A 73-year-old patient presents for and completes a tooth extraction. **After** the procedure, the patient discloses they are taking **81 mg baby aspirin** daily, prescribed following a myocardial infarction two years ago. The examiner is testing your ability to identify the post-disclosure problem AND apply **local measures only**.[1]

---

## Step 1 — Recognize the Core Issues

Two parallel problems must be addressed in your answer:

- **Patient communication concern:** Critical medical history was withheld/forgotten and only shared post-operatively
- **Clinical concern:** 81 mg aspirin is an antiplatelet agent that may affect hemostasis at the extraction socket[2]

> ⚠️ **Examiner's cue:** The word *"local"* in the question signals you do **not** need to discuss systemic interventions, physician referral, or medication cessation — only chairside measures.
> 

---

## Step 2 — Address the Withheld History (Patient Communication)

Handle this professionally and non-confrontationally:

- **Thank the patient** for sharing the information — better late than never
- **Do not blame or scold** — this creates distrust and may cause future withholding of information
- **Educate the patient** on why this information is critical: invasive procedures can cause bleeding, and knowing medications in advance allows you to adequately prepare and protect the patient
- **Reassure the patient** that this does not mean procedures would be refused or delayed — it means better care can be provided
- Emphasize they should proactively share **all medications and medical history** with **any** healthcare provider before any procedure, not just dental visits[2]

---

## Step 3 — Clinical Assessment & Local Hemostatic Measures

Evidence supports that 81 mg aspirin does **not** require cessation before dental extractions, and bleeding is manageable with local measures alone.[3][4][2]

Apply measures in a **stepwise, least-invasive-first** sequence:

| Step | Measure | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Firm pressure pack** (gauze) | First-line; have patient bite firmly for 30–45 min [3] |
| 2 | **Suturing the socket** | Figure-of-8 suture; indicated if pressure alone is insufficient [3] |
| 3 | **Local hemostatic agents** | Oxidized cellulose, collagen plug, gelatin sponge — placed in socket [2] |
| 4 | **Tranexamic acid–soaked gauze** | Use if above steps fail; antifibrinolytic, highly effective [2] |
| 5 | **Hemostatic dressing + observation** | Apply agent on gauze, seat patient in waiting area for 15–20 min before discharge [4] |

> **Do NOT discharge** until active hemostasis is visually confirmed — remove gauze and inspect the socket before the patient leaves.
> 

---

## Step 4 — Post-Operative Instructions

Give **both verbal and written** instructions. Minimum content to include:[2]

- **Bite firmly** on gauze for 30–45 minutes; replace if oozing restarts
- **Do not rinse, spit, or use straws** for the first 24 hours — protects the blood clot
- **Avoid hot liquids, vigorous activity**, and smoking
- If bleeding restarts at home, bite on fresh gauze firmly for 30–45 minutes
- **When to seek emergency care:** If bleeding is uncontrolled after 1–2 hours of sustained pressure, call the clinic or go to the emergency room; provide the clinic's after-hours contact number

---

## Step 5 — Documentation (Non-Negotiable)

Your progress note for this appointment **must clearly state:**

- Patient disclosed 81 mg aspirin use only **after** extraction was completed
- History may have been withheld or inadvertently forgotten (use neutral language — avoid accusatory wording)
- Patient was **educated** on the importance of disclosing medical history and medications prior to any dental procedure
- Local hemostatic measures taken (list exactly what was done)
- Hemostasis was **confirmed before discharge**
- Detailed post-operative instructions were given verbally and in writing
- Patient demonstrated understanding of when to contact the clinic or seek emergency care

> **Why this matters:** Thorough documentation protects you legally. If a complication arises later, your note demonstrates clinical due diligence despite incomplete pre-operative disclosure.[1]
> 

---

## Step 6 — What You Would NOT Do

Mentioning what you *would not* do makes your answer stand out to the examiner:

- **Would NOT** stop or alter the aspirin — evidence shows 81 mg/day does not require cessation for routine extractions, and the cardiovascular risk of stopping outweighs the minor bleeding risk[4][2]
- **Would NOT** seek physician consultation for this low-dose scenario — local measures are sufficient[2]
- **Would NOT** discharge the patient without visual confirmation of hemostasis
- **Would NOT** scold or create a confrontational interaction about the withheld history
- **Would NOT** administer systemic hemostatic agents for this scenario

---

## Quick Answer Structure (Exam Template)

Use this flow when writing your response:

1. **Summarize the scenario** in your own words (2–3 sentences)
2. **Flag the concern** — history shared post-operatively; acknowledge and educate patient
3. **List local hemostatic measures** in stepwise order
4. **State observation protocol** — do not discharge until hemostasis confirmed
5. **Post-op instructions** — verbal + written, include emergency contact criteria
6. **Documentation** — note history timing, education given, measures taken, discharge confirmation
7. **What you would NOT do** (bonus — makes the answer shine)

Sources
[1] NDECC https://ndeb-bned.ca/equivalency-process/ndecc-2/
[2] Dental Extraction Can Be Performed Safely in Patients on Aspirin ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4004018/
[3] The effect of aspirin on bleeding after extraction of teeth - PMC - NIH https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3722997/
[4] A longitudinal study to evaluate the bleeding pattern of patients on low dose aspirin therapy following dental extraction https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8140285/
[5] NDECC | Situational Judgement (SJ) Full Course https://jade.courses/course/ndecc-situational-judgement/
[6] NDECC® Situational Judgement Full Course - Prep Doctors https://ca.prepdoctors.com/courses/ndeb-dental-equivalency/ndecc-situational-judgement
[7] Exam Resources https://ndeb-bned.ca/resources/exam-resources/
[8] NDEB Exams for Foreign Trained Dentists https://www.scholarsdental.com/post/ndeb-canadian-dental-equivalency-exams-guide
[9] ® https://ndeb-bned.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/NDECC-Protocol_Effective-September-30-2024.pdf
[10] NDEB Pathway & Exam Prep | NDECC Clinical Skills ... https://dentalaspire.ca/ndecc
[11] NDECC ExamReady Program: Skills & Situational ... https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/ndeb-acs-ndecc
[12] Aspirin and bleeding in dentistry: an update and recommendations https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1079210407002296
[13] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[14] [PDF] Does aspirin need to be discontinued before an elective simple ... https://biomedicapk.com/files/articles/pdf/1176/show
[15] Situational Judgement Preparation Course For NDECC https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-for-ndecc

title: Caries risk for 11 year old
content:
The Case (Full Details)

---

- **Patient:** 11-year-old male child
- **Presenting concern:** Father noticed color changes in child's teeth
- **Clinical findings:** Several ICCMS scores 4 & 5 = **cavitated lesions**
- **Dietary history:** Frequently buys sugary snacks from school vending machine
- **Exam station type:** Health Promotion / Situational Judgement station

---

## Golden Rule (Never Skip This)

> *"No recommendation without diagnosis. No preventive recommendation without risk assessment."*
> 

**Always state the caries risk category before giving any plan.** Every recommendation depends on it.[1][2]

---

## Step 1 — Caries Risk Assessment

The ICCMS caries risk form applies to patients **≥6 years old**.[1]

### HIGH RISK Criteria (Any ONE = High Risk)

| # | Criterion | Key Detail |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Xerostomia / Severe dry mouth** | Any age |
| 2 | **Special Healthcare Need** | **Ages 6–14 ONLY** — a 35- or 50-year-old with the same condition = only Moderate Risk |
| 3 | **Chemotherapy or Radiotherapy (head/neck)** | Any age; hospital setting clue |
| 4 | **Frequent/prolonged sugar exposure between mealtimes** | NOT with meals — snacks, sugary drinks, sports drinks, energy drinks all count |
| 5 | **3 or more carious lesions OR restorations** | In last **36 months**; seen clinically or radiographically; could be amalgam, composite, anything |
| 6 | **1 tooth lost due to caries** | In last **36 months**; must NOT be from trauma or periodontal disease — even ONE tooth qualifies |
| 7 | **Caregiver with caries experience in last 6 months** | **ONLY for children aged 6–14** in the chair; doesn't apply to adults |

### MODERATE RISK Criteria (None of the above, but any of these)

Criterion

---

No established dental home

---

No fluoride exposure

---

Visible plaque or calculus in the mouth

---

Eating disorder (e.g., bulimia)

---

Any orthodontic appliance (fixed or removable), fixed bridge, or dental appliance

---

### LOW RISK

- None of the above criteria present
- **Rarely appears in exams** — there is little to recommend if the patient is already low risk[2]

---

## Memory Aid: The "ZERO" Image

This mental image helps quickly identify **High Risk** criteria during an exam:

- **Zero** = Xerostomia (severe dry mouth)
- **~14-year-old figure** = Special Healthcare Need (only ages 6–14)
- **Hospital background** = Chemotherapy / Radiotherapy
- **Snacks/chips in hand (not a meal)** = Frequent sugary food or drinks between meals
- **Magic number 3** = 3+ carious lesions or restorations in 36 months
- **One tooth shown** = 1 tooth lost due to caries in 36 months
- **Caregiver "sitting like a 6"** = Caregiver with caries in last **6** months (only for child aged 6–14)

---

## Step 2 — Apply to This Case

**→ HIGH RISK** because:

1. Several cavitated ICCMS 4 & 5 lesions = exceeds the "3 or more" criterion
2. Frequent sugary snacks from vending machine = sugar between mealtimes criterion[3][1]

**Write this at the top of your answer:** *"Based on the several ICCMS Class 4 and 5 cavitated lesions and frequent between-meal sugar exposure, this 11-year-old is categorized as High Risk for dental caries."*

---

## Step 3 — The 4 Ps (Mental Checklist Only)

| P | Stands For | Use |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **P1** | Paste (fluoride toothpaste) | Check: did I include toothpaste recommendation? |
| **P2** | Products (other products e.g., mouthwash) | Check: did I include other fluoride products? |
| **P3** | Practices (habits — diet, oral hygiene) | Check: did I address diet and behavior? |
| **P4** | Professional Care (in-office) | Check: did I include clinical treatment + recall? |

⚠️ **Do NOT write these as headings in your exam answer.** Use them mentally to check nothing is missed. Start with whatever the case highlights as the biggest problem.[1]

---

## Step 4 — Full Management Plan (High Risk, 11-Year-Old)

### A. Professional Care (Biggest Problem: Active Cavitated Lesions)

**1. Restorations (Definitive Treatment)**

- All cavitated lesions require restorations
- **Rationale:** Restoring today = preventing endodontic treatment tomorrow; leaving them = eventual pulp involvement → endo therapy. Restoration is still a *preventive* act — it prevents disease from escalating[3]

**2. Silver Diamine Fluoride (SDF) — Interim Option**

- If all restorations cannot be done at once (several lesions), apply SDF to arrest/control cavitated lesions in the meantime[4][5]
- Allows staged restoration: do 2, then 2, then 2, slowly
- **Requires parental consent** — must clearly inform of staining/darkening of treated teeth
- Use 38% SDF on cavitated lesions[4]

**3. Oral Prophylaxis (Professional Cleaning)**

- Clean the mouth thoroughly during the appointment
- Follow up immediately with fluoride varnish

**4. 5% Sodium Fluoride Varnish**

- Applied in-office after prophylaxis
- Provides topical fluoride protection[3]

**5. Recall & Radiographs**

- **Recall:** every **3 months** (High Risk)
- **Bitewing radiographs:** every **6 months** (High Risk)[1][3]
- ⚠️ This recall + bitewing interval is the ONE standard element every answer must include

---

### B. Practices — Dietary & Snacking Habits

**Core problem:** 11-year-old has unlimited access to school vending machine with sugary snacks.

**Counseling points for the parent:**

- Pack snacks from home — take away the reliance on vending machine
- Reduce pocket money / allowance to limit vending machine spending
- Implement a **"treat day" system** — e.g., Monday to Thursday bring from home, Friday only is treat day
- Encourage the child to form healthier snacking patterns (habits at 11 are harder to change than in toddlers, but still manageable with parental guidance)

**Drink recommendation:**

- Replace sugary drinks with **water**[3]

---

### C. Healthier Snacking Alternatives (Specifics for the Parent)

| ✅ Recommended | ❌ Avoid |
| --- | --- |
| **Cheese** (neutralizes acid) | Chips/crisps from vending machine |
| **Crunchy fruits** — apples | Soft/sticky fruits — grapes, oranges |
| **Crunchy vegetables** — carrots, celery, cucumbers | Sweets, chocolates, gummies |
| **Plain yogurt** | Fruit-at-the-bottom yogurt (equally sugary) |
| **Eggs** — hard-boiled, etc. | Sports/energy drinks (high sugar, frequent exposure) |
| **Water** | Carbonated/flavored drinks |

---

### D. Products — Home Fluoride

**1. Prescription-strength toothpaste: 5,000 ppm fluoride**

- Used **at least once daily, preferably at night**[6][7]
- Not necessarily lifelong — only during this high-risk period
- Replaces regular toothpaste for the high-risk period

**2. 0.2% Sodium Fluoride Mouthwash**

- Used **once a week**
- Safe for 11-year-olds[3]
- Alternatively: 0.05% NaF daily rinse

---

## Tangent: Sports/Energy Drinks (Exam Trap)

If a prompt says a 19-year-old drinks **1 litre of sports drink per day**:

- Training sessions (morning + evening = 2 + 2 hours) = drinking throughout training, not with a meal
- This counts as **frequent between-meal sugar exposure**
- → **High Risk** regardless of the patient's age
- Key skill: read implied information in the prompt and apply the right risk criteria[2]

---

## Special Age Rules — Critical for Tangent Questions

| Scenario | Risk |
| --- | --- |
| 11-year-old with special healthcare need | **HIGH Risk** |
| 35-year-old with broken arm / arthritis | **MODERATE Risk only** (special healthcare need does NOT apply >14 years) |
| 11-year-old whose caregiver had caries 3 months ago | **HIGH Risk** |
| 20-year-old whose caregiver had caries 3 months ago | **Does NOT apply** — caregiver rule is only for children 6–14 |

---

## Exam Answer Structure Template

> "Based on the information provided, the patient has several ICCMS Class 4 and 5 cavitated lesions and frequent exposure to sugar-containing snacks between mealtimes at school. Therefore, this 11-year-old falls under **High Risk for dental caries**. I would recommend the following plan of action to the father:"
> 

Then cover (in any logical order based on what the case emphasizes):

1. Restorations + SDF interim
2. Diet counseling + snacking alternatives
3. Oral prophylaxis + NaF varnish in-office
4. 5,000 ppm toothpaste at night
5. 0.2% NaF mouthwash weekly
6. Recall every 3 months + bitewing every 6 months
7. Optional: "The oral cavity is a snapshot of overall body health" — reinforces the importance of oral care to the parent[3]

---

## Practice Script (Simple English)

**"Good afternoon Mr. [Father's Name]. Thank you for bringing your son in today. You did the right thing by noticing the changes in his teeth. After examining his mouth, I found several cavities — holes that have already broken through the tooth surface. Because he has many active cavities and he's been having sugary snacks regularly at school, we classify him as high risk for tooth decay.**

**This means we need to act on two things — treat what's already there, and prevent more from forming.**

**For treatment: the cavities are too far along to heal on their own. We need to fill them. If we can't do all of them today, I'll apply a special silver solution — silver diamine fluoride — to freeze those cavities and stop them getting worse in the meantime. I'll need your permission for that, as it does cause some darkening of the treated teeth.**

**Today, I'll also clean his teeth professionally and apply a fluoride varnish to strengthen them.**

**For prevention at home: I want to prescribe a stronger fluoride toothpaste — 5,000 ppm. He should use this once a day, especially at night. I'll also suggest a fluoride mouthwash once a week. Both are safe for his age.**

**The biggest thing I need your help with is the snacking. Every time he eats something sugary between meals, acid attacks his teeth for about 20 minutes. If he's snacking from that vending machine every day, his teeth never get a chance to recover.**

**What I'd suggest is packing snacks from home. Great options are: cheese, apple slices, carrot sticks, celery, cucumber, plain yogurt, or hard-boiled eggs. These don't cause tooth decay. And his drink should be water — not juice or sports drinks.**

**You can still give him a treat day — say every Friday he can choose from the vending machine — but Monday to Thursday, snacks come from home. It also helps to limit how much pocket money he has for the machine.**

**We'll see him back in 3 months to check how he's doing, and take X-rays every 6 months to catch anything between the teeth early.**

**The mouth tells us a lot about a person's overall health, so getting this under control now will make a big difference for him going into his teenage years. Do you have any questions for me?"**

Sources
[1] ICCMS™ Quick Reference Guide for Practitioners and ... https://www.iccms-web.com/uploads/asset/5928404ea4df6343406124.pdf
[2] The International Caries Classification and Management ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4580843/
[3] Caries-Risk Assessment and Management for Infants, ... https://www.aapd.org/media/Policies_Guidelines/BP_CariesRiskAssessment.pdf
[4] Silver Diamine Fluoride (SDF) Efficacy in Arresting ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9566773/
[5] Silver diamine fluoride https://www.childcaries.sdcep.org.uk/guidance/dental-techniques/non-restorative-cavity-control/silver-diamine-fluoride/
[6] Preventive effect of high-fluoride dentifrice (5000 ppm) in ... https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20606431/
[7] Six things to consider when prescribing high-fluoride toothpastes to ... https://www.colgateprofessional.com/students-faculty/trending-topics/considerations-when-prescribing-prevident

title: Social media commission
content:
# NDECC Situational Judgment: Responding to a Product Endorsement Offer

## The Scenario

An oral hygiene appliance manufacturing company approaches you (a dentist) to:
1. **Promote their newly launched product** on your social media, including a **discount coupon** for your followers
2. **Pay you 10% commission** on revenue from every product sold through your referral

**Your answer: Politely but firmly decline the entire offer.** Two distinct ethical violations are at play — one relating to advertising guidelines, and one relating to conflict of interest.

***

## Issue 1: Breach of Professional Advertising Guidelines

Promoting a product on social media with a discount coupon directed at your followers constitutes **inappropriate social media promotion** in a healthcare context.

- Professional advertising guidelines explicitly prohibit promoting **coupons, discounts, freebies, giveaways, and competitions** in a healthcare setting[1][2]
- Patients must be encouraged to seek treatment or acquire appliances based on **clinical need**, not because a product is discounted[2]
- Appropriate use of social media for a dental professional is restricted to **educational content**, case discussion (with consent), and oral health awareness — not commercial endorsements[3][2]
- Permissible social media activity includes sharing oral hygiene education and clinical information; it does **not** include targeted discount offers to a follower base[2]

> **Key Principle:** Promoting a product with a financial incentive (discount/coupon) to followers conflates a professional platform with commercial advertising and misleads patients into acquiring products based on price, not clinical need.

***

## Issue 2: Financial Conflict of Interest
