Accepting a **10% revenue commission** per product referred/sold creates a direct and serious conflict of interest.

### What is a Conflict of Interest?
A conflict of interest arises when a **personal financial gain** competes with your **fiduciary duty** to the patient. As a dentist, you are bound by a fiduciary relationship — the patient's best interest must always come first, above any personal or financial consideration.[4][5]

### Why This Commission Creates a Problem

| Scenario | Without Commission | With Commission |
|---|---|---|
| Patient A needs this specific appliance | Recommend it on clinical merit ✅ | Recommend it on clinical merit ✅ (incidental 10% gain) |
| Patient B would benefit more from a different product | Recommend the better option ✅ | Bias toward this company's product for financial gain ❌ |

- When Patient B presents with a case where a **different appliance** (e.g., from another lab/supplier) would be clinically superior, the commission creates a subconscious — or overt — financial incentive to still recommend the commission-generating product[5]
- Over time, this **blinds clinical judgment**: the dentist may stop differentiating between patient requirements and simply default to the commissioned product for every patient[4]
- The harm is not always immediate or intentional — but the ethical breach lies in the **possibility** of compromised judgment, not only in proven misconduct[6][5]

### The Commission Cannot Be Redirected Either
The arrangement remains an ethical violation **regardless of who receives the money**:[6][4]
- You cannot redirect the 10% to your **spouse**
- You cannot have it paid to your **dental corporation**
- You cannot channel it through any **third party**

The underlying principle is unchanged: a financial arrangement tied to product referral compromises the independence of clinical decision-making.[4][6]

### Fee-Splitting Is Also Prohibited
This same principle applies in other referral relationships:[4]
- You **cannot** tell a specialist: "I'll refer patients to you, give me 10% of what you earn"
- You **cannot** tell a dental lab: "For every patient I send your way, give me a cut"
- All such arrangements violate the prohibition on **fee-splitting and rebates** under dental codes of ethics[6][4]

***

## How to Respond to the Company

**Step 1 — Acknowledge and identify the issues:**
Recognize that this offer presents **two separate ethical violations**: inappropriate advertising and financial conflict of interest.

**Step 2 — Decline the commission arrangement:**
- Politely inform the company that you **cannot accept any percentage of revenue** linked to product referrals
- Explain your **fiduciary duty** to patients — clinical recommendations must be made purely on professional judgment and evidence-based review, not financial incentive
- Clarify that you may still independently evaluate their product on its merits; if found clinically suitable for a specific patient's needs, you may recommend it — but **without any commission arrangement in place**

**Step 3 — Decline the social media promotion:**
- Inform the company that promoting their product with a discount coupon to your followers is a **direct breach of professional advertising guidelines**
- Your social media presence may only be used for **educational purposes**, and not for commercial product promotion with financial inducements (discounts, giveaways, etc.)[2]

**Step 4 — Offer a compliant alternative (optional):**
- You can mention that if, after your own **independent evidence-based clinical review**, their product is found to benefit patients, you may discuss it in a clinical context with relevant patients on a **case-by-case basis** — with no commission, no social media promotion, and based solely on clinical need

***

## Core Exam Takeaways

- **Fiduciary duty** = patient's best interest always comes first, above any financial or personal gain
- **Conflict of interest** = any monetary arrangement tied to product/referral recommendation that may — even potentially — compromise clinical judgment
- **Advertising guidelines** = no coupons, discounts, freebies, giveaways, or competitions in healthcare promotion
- **Redirection of funds** (to spouse, corporation, third party) does **not** remove the ethical breach
- The correct response is a **polite but firm decline**, with a clear explanation of both violations
- You may still use a product if clinically justified — but purely on merit, with **zero financial ties** to the manufacturer[5][4]

Sources
[1] [PDF] Guideline: Advertising https://cdho.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/GUI-Advertising.pdf
[2] Social Media Advertising for Dentists: Compliance Tips https://completesmilesbv.com.au/social-media-advertising-for-dentists-compliance-tips/
[3] Social Media Policies for Dentists https://www.ada.org/resources/practice/practice-management/social-media-policies-for-dentists
[4] Illegal and Unethical Referral Fees and Fee Splitting – Michigan Dental Association https://www.michigandental.org/legal-article/illegal-and-unethical-referral-fees-and-fee-splitting/
[5] [PDF] Guide to Standard of Practice: Conflict of Interest https://denturists-cdo.com/Resources/Guidelines/Guide-to-the-Standard-of-Practice-Conflict-of-Int.aspx
[6] Problems With Referral Incentives Explained | Kerr Russell - JDSupra https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/problems-with-referral-incentives-4258155/
[7] Ads And Boosted Posts https://hibu.com/blog/industries/25-dental-social-media-post-ideas-that-actually-attract-patients
[8] 36 Brilliant Dental Marketing Social Media Ideas for 2025 https://mysocialpractice.com/2025/11/dental-marketing-social-media/
[9] Dentist-NDEB Examinations & Certification https://internationalhealthprofessionals.ca/dentist-ndeb-examinations-certification/
[10] Dental Office Social Media Marketing Ideas https://mconsent.net/blog/dental-social-media-marketing-guide/
[11] [PDF] Code of Professional Conduct Malaysian Dental Council https://mpdpa.org/images/Code%20of%20Professional%20Conduct%202022.pdf
[12] Dental Social Media... https://www.carecredit.com/providers/insights/dental-social-media-best-practices/
[13] What is NDECC? https://www.confidentist.ca/faq-items/what-is-ndecc/
[14] Advertising Guidelines https://www.rcdso.org/en-ca/standards-guidelines-resources/standards-guidelines-advisories/advertising-guidelines
[15] [PDF] Informed Consent and Disclosure - Issues in Dental Ethics https://www.dentalethics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/jacd-74-2-parker-satkoske.pdf

title: MMM3 19 year old brother bring 12 yr old
content:
19 year old brother took his younger brother to clinic, as his 12 year old young brother got tooth 1.1 avulsed and lost due to a trauma at school. it happened yesterday. their parents are on vacant in turkey for 2 months. and you found another caries in 16O, what to do
In an NDECC simulator station, this scenario tests your understanding of legal consent, trauma management protocols, and age-appropriate treatment planning. Here are the main points you must discuss with the 19-year-old brother:

Consent and Legal Authority
Since the 12-year-old is a minor and likely incapable of consenting to complex prosthodontic trauma treatment, informed consent must be obtained from a Substitute Decision Maker (SDM). Under Canadian guidelines (such as the Health Care Consent Act), because the parents are completely unavailable, the 19-year-old brother (being over 16) is legally authorized to act as the SDM and sign the consent forms. You should still document that the parents are away and ideally offer to phone them to keep them informed, but the brother has the legal right to proceed.

Medical and Trauma Assessment
Before looking at the teeth, you must rule out severe medical emergencies. Ask the brother if the child lost consciousness, threw up, or experienced dizziness after the accident to rule out a concussion or head injury. If head trauma is suspected, he must be referred to a hospital immediately. You must also ask if the child's tetanus immunization is up-to-date, as the trauma occurred outdoors/at school and the socket was exposed.

Clinical and Radiographic Exam
Explain that you need to take x-rays (Periapical and Occlusal) to ensure no root fragments were left behind in the bone, and to check for alveolar bone fractures. You will also examine the lips and cheeks for any embedded tooth fragments, and do baseline vitality testing on the neighboring teeth (1.2 and 2.1) to see if their nerves were damaged by the impact.
​

Managing the Lost Tooth (1.1)
Since the tooth is completely lost and it has been over 24 hours, replantation is impossible. You must explain the following long-term and short-term plans:

No Implants Yet: Emphasize that because the patient is only 12, his jaw is still growing. Placing a dental implant or a permanent fixed bridge now is strictly contraindicated as it will sink and interfere with facial growth. He must wait until growth is complete (around age 18-21) for a permanent implant.

Transitional Prosthesis: To maintain the space (so other teeth don't shift) and restore his smile, you will fabricate a temporary replacement. The most appropriate options are a removable "flipper" (acrylic partial denture) or an Essix retainer with a fake tooth inside.

Managing the Cavity (16O)
Inform the brother that you found a cavity on the upper right molar (16O). Emphasize that because it is painless, it is not an emergency. Today's priority is exclusively managing the trauma and the missing front tooth. You will create a treatment plan to restore the cavity with a simple filling at a future, separate appointment.

Post-Operative Care and Follow-up
Provide instructions for a soft diet and chlorhexidine mouthwash to keep the healing socket clean. Explain that the neighboring teeth suffered trauma as well and their nerves could silently die over time. Strict follow-up appointments (at 1 week, 3 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months) are mandatory to re-test the vitality of the adjacent teeth and monitor the healing of the bone.

Do you have the contact information for your parents in Turkey so we can attempt to update them on the treatment plan?

Prepared using Gemini 3.1 Pro Thinking
what happens if they have or don't have
In an NDECC scenario, whether the 19-year-old brother has the parents' contact information directly dictates who has the legal authority to consent to the 12-year-old's dental treatment. This is governed by the Health Care Consent Act and the legal hierarchy of Substitute Decision Makers (SDMs).

If He Has the Contact Information
Under the SDM hierarchy, parents rank higher than siblings. A lower-ranking person (the brother) cannot give consent if a higher-ranking person (the parents) is "available". If the brother has their contact information, you are legally required to attempt to reach the parents in Turkey. You must obtain verbal informed consent from them over the phone, explain the trauma findings, and document the conversation carefully in the patient's chart before proceeding with any treatment.

If He Does Not Have the Contact Information
If the parents are completely unreachable or their contact information is unknown, they are legally considered "unavailable". In this situation, the legal authority to provide consent drops down to the next highest-ranking available person on the SDM hierarchy. Since the brother is over 16 years old and mentally capable, he legally becomes the Substitute Decision Maker. You can accept his written consent for the trauma assessment, x-rays, and the transitional prosthesis without violating any ethical or legal guidelines.

Documentation Requirements
Regardless of the outcome, you must clearly verbalize to the examiner and document in the chart exactly why you are accepting consent from a specific person. If you proceed with the brother's consent, your records must state that the parents were out of the country, unreachable, and therefore unavailable to act as the primary SDM.

title: DSA always late
content:
## Core scenario

- You have a **dental assistant** (DA) with whom you have had a very good working relationship for a long time.[4]
- For the last 3 months, the DA has started arriving late for shifts **consistently** (from about 10 minutes late to now up to 30 minutes late).[4]
- They give no proper explanation, only “sorry I’m late”, and the day just continues without discussion.  
- You have sometimes covered them by delaying your morning patients, but now your **schedule and patient care are being affected**.[4]
- The lateness has reached a point where it can impact **duty of care**: delayed appointments, rushed procedures, possible omissions, reduced quality of care.[2][4]

Key exam message: This is a professionalism and problem‑solving question, not a punishment question.[3][2]

***

## What is NOT appropriate (critical errors)

These are approaches that would be marked as **unprofessional / not competent** in SJ:

- Immediately giving written warnings, final warnings, or official reprimands without first understanding the reason.  
- Escalating straight away to the practice manager or owner without first speaking directly to the DA.  
- Firing the assistant, or saying you would “get rid of” or “replace” them as the first step.  
- Using threatening language: “If you’re late again, I will report/fire you.”  
- Assuming the assistant is “taking advantage of you” or is lazy, without exploring reasons.  
- Doing nothing: ignoring the pattern, “waiting to see if it gets worse”, or saying you will wait another few months even though patient care is already affected.  

All of the above show poor professionalism, poor communication, and poor conflict management, and will score low in NDECC SJ.[6][7][2][4]

***

## Professional principles to demonstrate

The station is really testing these principles:

- **Professionalism & duty of care**  
  - Patient care and safety must remain the top priority.  
  - Lateness is important because it impacts patients and the clinic workflow, not because of personal irritation.[1][2][4]

- **Right message, right person**  
  - Address the issue directly with the DA involved, not via gossip, other staff, or immediate escalation.[7][6]

- **Safe, non‑judgmental communication**  
  - Create a private, calm, safe space for conversation.  
  - Use active listening, avoid blame, and clarify that the goal is solution‑finding, not punishment.[8][6][7]

- **Problem‑solving approach**  
  - Identify the **root cause** of the lateness.  
  - Explore reasonable solutions that protect patient care and respect the assistant’s situation.[6][7]

- **Timely intervention**  
  - Do not ignore the pattern once it starts affecting patient care.  
  - Intervene early in a constructive way.[2][4]

***

## Step‑by‑step professional approach

### 1. Acknowledge the seriousness

- Recognize that the repeated lateness over several weeks/months now has the **capacity to impact duty of care** (delayed starts, rushed appointments, possible missed steps).[2][4]
- Understand that it is now appropriate and necessary to **intervene** professionally.

### 2. Plan a private, calm meeting

- Choose a **private** space in the clinic (office/quiet room).  
- Set the tone as **non‑judgmental and respectful**, one professional speaking to another.  
- Do not confront in front of patients or other staff.  

Example opening:  
“I’ve noticed you’ve been arriving late quite often recently, and I’m concerned because it’s starting to affect our patient schedule. I’d like to understand what’s going on so we can find a solution together.”

### 3. Invite the assistant to explain (find the “why”)

- Clearly say that the purpose is to **understand the reason**, not to blame or threaten.  
- Use open questions and active listening:  
  - “Can you help me understand what has been causing the delays in the mornings?”  
  - “Has something changed recently in your routine or personal situation?”[7][8][6]

- You may acknowledge possible causes (just as examples, not assumptions):  
  - Childcare responsibilities and morning school drop‑offs.  
  - Living far away and relying on public transport, missing the early bus.  
  - Seasonal/weather‑related delays (e.g., winter, snow, icy roads).  
  - Short‑term issues such as car breakdown or repair.  

The key: you **do not know** the reason yet; your job is to give them a safe space to share it.

### 4. Explain the impact on patients and clinic

- After listening, gently explain **why** punctuality matters:  
  - Morning appointments start late, so the whole day runs behind.  
  - You may have to rush or shorten appointments.  
  - There is risk of missing steps, reducing quality of care.  
  - Other patients and staff are affected and may become frustrated.[9][10][11][2]

- Emphasize that this is about **patient care and clinic needs**, not a personal preference or personality clash.  

Example:  
“When we start late, it pushes every appointment back, and I worry that the care we provide may become rushed. My priority is that our patients receive safe, unhurried treatment.”

### 5. Collaborate on realistic solutions

Once you understand the cause, work **with** the assistant to find a practical solution. Your answer can give a couple of concrete examples to show problem‑solving:

- If the issue is long‑term/structural (e.g., school drop‑offs):  
  - Consider **adjusting their schedule** permanently so they start later.  
  - Arrange another assistant or staff member to cover early appointments.  

- If the issue is commute distance or bus times:  
  - Adjust their start time to match a reliable bus.  
  - Temporarily shift them to later shifts; have another team member cover early hours.  

- If the issue is temporary (e.g., car in repair):  
  - Arrange **temporary coverage** for the first hour (another assistant, temp agency, or extra staff).  
  - Give them a slightly later temporary start until the car issue is resolved.  

- If the issue is seasonal/weather‑related:  
  - Agree on leaving home earlier during that season if possible.  
  - Adjust start times slightly or provide flexibility during severe weather.  

The key SJ message: you are **solution‑focused**, not trying to eliminate the assistant.

### 6. Set clear expectations and follow up

- Once a plan is agreed, clearly restate expectations around punctuality and how the solution will work.  
- Confirm that the assistant understands that lateness must improve because of patient care and team impact.  
- Arrange a follow‑up check after some time (e.g., a few weeks) to see if the solution is working.  

This shows you are professional, fair, and still prioritizing duty of care.[6][7][2]

***

## How to phrase your written exam answer (template)

You can adapt this template for the SJ written response:

1. **Recognize the impact**  
   - “The assistant’s repeated late arrivals over the past three months are now affecting patient scheduling and have the potential to compromise duty of care. Therefore, I need to intervene in a professional and timely manner.”

2. **State that you will address it directly and privately**  
   - “I would arrange a private, calm meeting with the dental assistant to discuss the issue. I would ensure a safe, non‑judgmental environment where they feel comfortable to speak openly.”

3. **Clarify your goal (understanding + solution, not punishment)**  
   - “I would explain that the purpose of the conversation is to understand the reasons behind their lateness and to work together to find a solution that protects patient care.”

4. **Describe how you will explore the reasons**  
   - “Using open questions and active listening, I would invite them to share any underlying factors, such as childcare responsibilities, transportation issues, or temporary personal difficulties.”

5. **Explain how you will link it to patient care**  
   - “I would respectfully explain how their lack of punctuality is impacting our schedule and potentially the quality of care, emphasizing that this is not a personal criticism but a concern for our patients and the clinic.”

6. **Offer potential solutions**  
   - “Depending on the cause, we could consider adjusting their shift times, arranging another assistant to cover early appointments, or organizing temporary coverage while a short‑term problem (e.g., car repair) is resolved.”

7. **Clarify what you will not do**  
   - “I would not immediately reprimand, threaten, or fire the assistant, nor would I ignore the issue. Instead, I would address it collaboratively and professionally, focusing on patient‑centered care and maintaining a positive working relationship.”

8. **Mention follow‑up**  
   - “Finally, I would agree on clear expectations moving forward and schedule a follow‑up to ensure that the plan is effective and that patient care is no longer affected.”

***

## Quick revision points for this scenario

- Good past relationship + new problem = **supportive, not punitive** approach.  
- First action = **private conversation with the assistant**, not manager, not firing.  
- Focus on **root cause**, active listening, empathy.  
- Always link your concern to **duty of care and patient impact**.  
- Show **solution‑oriented** thinking (schedule changes, coverage, temporary adjustments).  
- Avoid critical errors: threats, punishment, immediate escalation, or doing nothing.[5][1][2][4]

Would it help if I turn this into a 1‑page bullet summary you can print and memorize for SJ revision?

Sources
[1] NDECC​® - The National Dental Examining Board of Canada https://ndeb-bned.ca/equivalency-process/ndecc-2/
[2] Beyond The Basics: A Deep Dive Into NDECC's Situational Judgement https://www.wisesouls.ca/post/judgement-day-a-deep-dive-into-ndecc-s-situational-judgement
[3] Course | Situational Judgment https://passingplandental.ca/Courses/CourseDetail/3
[4] What is NDECC? https://www.confidentist.ca/faq-items/what-is-ndecc/
[5] NDECC Situational Judgement - Acecourses https://acecourses.ca/courses/situational-judgement
[6] Conflict management in healthcare: effective practices and ... https://www.safeteam.academy/en/article/resolution-conflits-sante
[7] Conflict Resolution Techniques for Healthcare Teams https://www.halostaffing.co.uk/conflict-resolution-techniques-for-healthcare-teams/
[8] Two staff members are clashing in your healthcare team. How do you facilitate a constructive conversation? https://www.linkedin.com/advice/3/two-staff-members-clashing-your-healthcare-violc
[9] How to Manage Late Patients: A Guide for Busy Practices - DocVA https://docva.com/how-to-manage-late-patients/
[10] The right way to handle chronically late dental patients - https://ascentdentalsolutions.com/the-right-way-to-handle-chronically-late-dental-patients/
[11] Late Arrival Policy for Patients: How to Deal with Tardy ... https://www.dentalintel.com/blog-posts/late-arrival-policy-for-patients-how-to-deal-with-tardy-patients-at-your-dental-practice
[12] Situational Judgement Preparation Course For NDECC - ITDOnline https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-for-ndecc
[13] FREE First Lecture | NDECC Situational Judgement Course - YouTube https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1B7Xwpkn2KQ
[14] NDECC | Situational Judgement (SJ) Full Course https://jade.courses/course/ndecc-situational-judgement/
[15] Late Again! Retraining Patients and Your Team for Better ... https://www.dentalmanagers.com/blog/retraining-patients-and-your-team/

title: Hiv patient
content:
Got it — you’re asking for a clean, structured version of your HIV patient management communication station (for NDSE/OSCE-style revision), keeping all the original clinical and ethical points but in a clear, organized way.  

Here’s a well-organized outline you can use for quick reference and practice:

***

### Communication Station: HIV-Positive Patient Requiring Extraction

**Scenario Summary:**  
A 43-year-old female, long-term but irregular dental patient, presents with pain requiring tooth extraction. On updating her medical history, she reveals that she was diagnosed with HIV a year ago and is under antiretroviral therapy with good control.

***

### Step 1: Establish Rapport and Acknowledge Disclosure
- Thank the patient for sharing their HIV status.  
- Maintain a calm, non-judgmental, and professional demeanor.  
- Avoid showing surprise, shock, or discomfort.  
- Reassure confidentiality — the information will stay within her clinical records and guide appropriate care.

> “Thank you for letting me know about your diagnosis. It helps me plan the safest and most effective care for you.”

***

### Step 2: Update Medical and Dental History
- Take a concise but complete medical history:
  - Duration since diagnosis (1 year)
  - Current medications (antiretroviral regimen)
  - Most recent CD4 count and viral load, if known
  - History of opportunistic infections or hospitalizations  
- Update dental records accordingly before proceeding with treatment.

***

### Step 3: Pain Management and Current Complaint
- Acknowledge the patient’s pain and need for extraction.  
- Assess whether extraction is urgent or can be scheduled.  
- Proceed with standard dental management following **universal precautions**.

***

### Step 4: Universal Precautions and Infection Control
- Use **standard infection control measures** – these are sufficient for all patients:
  - Gloves, masks, protective eyewear, sterilization, disinfection.
- **Do NOT**:
  - Double glove.
  - Schedule the patient at the end of the day.
  - Segregate instruments or workspace.
- Treat this patient the same as any other, applying *universal* rather than discriminatory precautions.

***

### Step 5: Ethical and Professional Conduct
- Provide care in a **non-discriminatory, empathetic, and respectful** manner.  
- Avoid stigmatizing behavior or unnecessary referrals.  
- Denying, postponing, or transferring care purely due to HIV status is unethical and considered a **critical error**.

***

### Step 6: Patient Education and Counseling
- Reassure the patient that dental treatment is safe and effective under standard infection control.
- Address potential fear or stigma that may have caused irregular attendance.
- Encourage **regular dental visits** to:
  - Detect early oral manifestations of HIV (e.g. candidiasis, ulcers, hairy leukoplakia, etc.).
  - Manage oral health proactively and maintain overall well-being.
- Emphasize interaction between oral health and systemic control.

> “Regular visits can help us identify and manage any early mouth changes related to your treatment or health, preventing pain like this in the future.”

***

### Step 7: Reinforcement of Interprofessional Care
- Encourage coordination with her physician for ongoing updates (with consent).
- Note that well-controlled HIV patients respond similarly to others in dental treatment.

***

### Step 8: Summary of Key Principles
- Provide **full routine dental care** under standard precautions.  
- Ensure **ethical, stigma-free** behavior.  
- Encourage **regular attendance** for oral health monitoring.  
- Avoid **critical errors** such as:
  - Refusing treatment.  
  - Rescheduling solely based on HIV.  
  - Double gloving or excessive precautions.  
  - Providing antibiotics without justification and dismissing the patient.

***

### Sample Closing Line (for OSCE communication)
> “I want to reassure you that your condition will not affect the quality of care you receive here. We’ll proceed with your extraction today and make sure you’re comfortable. I’ll also encourage you to come in for regular check-ups so we can monitor your oral health together.”

***

Would you like me to format this into a **ready-to-practice OSCE script** (with examiner cues, communication steps, and marking focus points)? It would be ideal for quick study and mock-station rehearsal.

Sources

title: Torn glove
content:
Here is a fully organized, structured study guide for this **NDECC Situational Judgement (SJ) scenario** — covering the IPAC breach with a newly hired associate:

***

# NDECC SJ Exam: IPAC Breach — Associate Wore Torn Gloves

## Scenario Overview

A **newly hired dental associate** performed an extraction and continued working throughout the procedure with a **torn/breached glove**, only discarding it after the procedure was complete.  You, as the **clinic owner/supervisor**, were informed of this by an **assistant** (third party). Your role is to address this with the associate directly.[1]

***

## Why This Is a Problem

This is an **Infection Prevention and Control (IPAC) violation** — a breach that should be immediately recognized.  Key risks include:[2]

- Transmission of infection **from patient to dentist** (and vice versa)
- Exposure to bloodborne pathogens, saliva, and other contaminants
- **Torn/holed gloves** are explicitly listed as an infection control error under NDECC protocol[3][1]
- Continuing a procedure with breached PPE **jeopardizes patient and operator safety** — which can rise to a critical error level if a clear lack of understanding is demonstrated[1]

***

## Your Response Framework (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Greet & Set the Tone
- Greet the associate warmly and professionally
- Keep tone **non-punitive** — this is a teaching moment, not a disciplinary hearing
- Remember: in the SJ exam, the simulator **plays the associate**, so connect with them first

### Step 2 — Gather Information from the Right Source
- Do **not** assume guilt; always verify from the person directly
- Open with something like:
> *"It has come to my attention that during today's extraction procedure, there may have been a breach in your glove and you continued working. I wanted to hear from you directly — can you tell me what happened?"*
- Allow the associate (simulator) to **confirm** the incident
- This step demonstrates fairness and proper conflict management[2]

### Step 3 — Acknowledge & Normalize (Non-Judgmentally)
- Acknowledge what they said: *"Thank you for confirming that"*
- State that this is unfortunate, and that IPAC is **standard office policy** — not optional, not new, not changing
- Avoid making it personal; frame it as a system/protocol issue

### Step 4 — Explain the Importance of PPE & IPAC

Cover the following points clearly:

- **PPE** (gloves, mask, gown/drape, eyewear) creates a **protective barrier** limiting the spread of infection between patient and operator[4]
- Every component matters — a breach in **any one element** (torn glove, improperly worn mask, no eye protection) **reduces overall protection**
- IPAC protocols exist to protect **both the patient and the dental team** from transmissible diseases[2]
- The office has a **zero-tolerance policy** for IPAC violations

### Step 5 — Explain the Correct Protocol (What Should Have Been Done)

The associate should have:

1. **Immediately recognized** the torn glove
2. **Stopped the procedure**
3. **Removed the torn gloves**
4. Performed **hand hygiene**:
   - If hands are **visibly soiled** → wash with running water + antibacterial soap
   - If hands are **not visibly soiled** → antibacterial hand rub is acceptable
5. **Donned new gloves**
6. **Resumed the procedure**[4][2]

> ⚠️ Failing to tell the associate **what to do** (the correct protocol steps above) would be considered a **critical error** in the SJ exam.

### Step 6 — Explain Consequences of Repeated Violations
- IPAC violations that are **deliberate and repeated** can be classified as **professional misconduct**
- The office cannot allow the practice to put patients or staff at risk of infection transmission

### Step 7 — Invite Questions & Offer Support

End the conversation by:

- Asking: *"Do you have any questions for me?"*
- Directing them to the **office IPAC manual** (provide page reference if available)
- Offering a **hands-on retraining session** on infection prevention and control
- Mentioning that you will **review PPE protocol at the next team meeting** (frame it as a routine team refresh — not a public call-out of the associate)

***

## Critical Errors to Avoid in Your SJ Response

| What You Must NOT Do | Why It's a Problem |
|---|---|
| Skip gathering info from the associate directly | Violates fair process; shows poor conflict management |
| Dive straight into correction without confirmation | Assumes guilt; breaks rapport with simulator |
| Forget to explain the **correct PPE breach steps** | This is a **critical error** — exam specifically tests this knowledge |
| Only discuss gloves, not full PPE | Incomplete IPAC answer; breach of any PPE element matters |
| Fail to offer follow-up support/resources | Answer is incomplete without remediation plan |

***

## Key IPAC Concepts to Remember

- **PPE components:** Gloves, mask, gown/drape, protective eyewear[4]
- **Gloves with tears or holes** = listed infection control error under NDECC protocol[1]
- **Demonstrating clear lack of IPAC understanding** = critical error[3]
- The **order to don PPE**: Gown → Mask → Eye protection → Gloves[4]
- The **order to doff PPE** (remove): reverse order, followed immediately by hand hygiene[4]

***

## Bonus Points (Yellow-Highlighted Tips from Instructor)

These are *not mandatory* but elevate your answer:
- Offer to schedule a **formal IPAC retraining session**
- Mention reviewing IPAC at the **next team meeting** as a group refresh
- Reference the **specific IPAC manual** section for self-directed learning
- Reinforce that this is about **office culture of safety**, not blame

Here are full, detailed **practice scripts** for this NDECC SJ scenario. Two versions are provided — a **full elaborate script** and a **concise script** — so you can practice both.

***

# NDECC SJ Practice Scripts
## Scenario: Associate Wore Torn Gloves During Extraction

***

## Full Elaborate Script (Aim for This)

***

### 🔵 Opening — Greet & Set the Tone

> **"Good afternoon [Associate's name], thank you for taking the time to meet with me. I wanted to have a quick chat with you about something that came to my attention today. Please have a seat — this is just a conversation, and I want to make sure we're both on the same page moving forward."**

***

### 🔵 Gather Information — Confirm from the Right Source

> **"So, it has come to my attention — through one of the assistants — that during your extraction procedure earlier today, there may have been a breach in your glove, and that the procedure continued without changing the gloves at the time of the breach. I always prefer to hear things directly from the person involved, so I wanted to ask you — can you tell me what happened from your perspective?"**

*(Pause — allow simulator/associate to confirm. They will say: "Yes, my glove tore during the procedure, but I finished the extraction and then discarded the gloves after.")*

> **"Thank you for being open with me about that. I appreciate you sharing that."**

***

### 🔵 Acknowledge the Breach — Connect to Office Policy

> **"I do want to address this, because as you know, our office follows very strict Infection Prevention and Control protocols — what we refer to as IPAC. This isn't something new or something that changes — it is standard, non-negotiable office policy, and it applies to everyone on our team, including myself."**

> **"What happened today — continuing a procedure with a torn glove — is considered an IPAC breach, and unfortunately, it is something that should not have occurred."**

***

### 🔵 Explain Why PPE Matters

> **"Let me take a moment to explain why this is so important. Personal Protective Equipment — our PPE — includes our gloves, mask, protective eyewear, and gown or drape. Each of these elements works together as a system to create a protective barrier between the operator and the patient."**

> **"The goal is to prevent the transmission of infection in both directions — from the patient to the dentist, and from the dentist to the patient. We are working in an environment where we are constantly exposed to blood, saliva, and potentially transmissible pathogens. PPE is our front line of defence."**

> **"Now, here is something very important to understand — it is not just about the gloves. If there is a breach in any one component — a torn glove, an improperly fitted mask, no eye protection — the entire level of protection provided by the PPE system is compromised. So we have to be mindful of the adequacy of all our PPE throughout every procedure."**

***

### 🔵 Explain the Correct Protocol — What Should Have Happened

> **"So, let me walk you through what the correct steps would have been the moment you noticed the tear in your glove."**

> **"The first thing to do is to stop the procedure immediately. As soon as a breach is identified, you stop — even mid-procedure if needed."**

> **"Next, you remove the torn gloves right away. After removing the gloves, you must perform hand hygiene. If your hands are visibly soiled with blood or debris, you wash your hands thoroughly with running water and antibacterial soap. If your hands are not visibly soiled, an antibacterial hand rub is also acceptable."**

> **"Once hand hygiene is done, you don new, fresh gloves. And only then do you resume the procedure."**

> **"So the sequence is: Stop → Remove gloves → Hand hygiene → New gloves → Resume. It is a straightforward protocol, but it is absolutely critical that it is followed every single time."**

***

### 🔵 Address Consequences — Keep It Professional, Not Threatening

> **"I also want you to be aware that IPAC violations are taken very seriously — not just by our office, but at a professional and regulatory level. Breaches that are deliberate or that are repeated can be classified as professional misconduct. That is something that could have serious implications for your career."**

> **"Our office cannot afford to put patients at risk of infection transmission, and we equally do not want to put you or any member of our team at that risk either. So this is something we take a zero-tolerance approach to."**

***

### 🔵 Close — Invite Questions & Offer Support

> **"I want to make sure you feel supported going forward, so I do have a couple of suggestions for you."**

> **"First, I'd encourage you to review our office IPAC manual — I'll mark the relevant section for you. It outlines all of our infection prevention protocols in detail, and it's a great reference."**

> **"Second, if you'd like, I am happy to arrange a hands-on retraining session on IPAC and proper PPE use. There's absolutely no shame in that — it's just making sure we're all aligned."**

> **"And lastly, I do want to mention — we have a team meeting coming up, and I'm going to take the opportunity to do a group review of our PPE protocols with the whole team. We have some new members on board, and I think it's always good to refresh everyone together. This isn't about singling anyone out — it's just good practice."**

> **"Do you have any questions for me at this point?"**

*(Pause for response.)*

> **"Great. Thank you again for speaking with me openly about this. I know it wasn't an easy conversation, but I really do appreciate it. Let's make sure we move forward with this in mind. Take care."**

***
***

## Concise Script (Minimum Acceptable Version)

***

> **"Hi [name], thanks for meeting with me. I heard from the assistant that your glove may have torn during the extraction today and the procedure continued without changing it. I wanted to hear it from you directly — is that what happened?"**

*(Confirm — yes.)*

> **"Thank you. I do need to address this because it is an IPAC breach, and our office follows strict infection prevention and control protocols. PPE — our gloves, mask, eyewear, and gown — protects both you and the patient from transmissible infections. A breach in any part of that system compromises the protection we're providing."**

> **"What should have happened: the moment the glove tore, you stop, remove the torn gloves, perform hand hygiene — soap and water if visibly soiled, hand rub if not — and then put on new gloves before continuing."**

> **"Repeated or deliberate IPAC breaches can be considered professional misconduct, so this is something we take very seriously. I'd like you to review the IPAC section in our office manual, and I'm also happy to schedule a retraining session for you if that would help."**

> **"Do you have any questions for me? No? Great — let's make sure this doesn't happen again. Thank you."**

***

## Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

| Stage | Key Line to Say |
|---|---|
| **Open** | *"I want to hear it from you directly — is that what happened?"* |
| **Acknowledge** | *"This is a breach of our IPAC protocol — standard, non-negotiable office policy"* |
| **PPE importance** | *"PPE prevents infection in both directions — patient to dentist, dentist to patient"* |
| **Correct steps** | *"Stop → Remove gloves → Hand hygiene → New gloves → Resume"* |
| **Hand hygiene rule** | *"Visibly soiled = soap + running water; Not soiled = hand rub is acceptable"* |
| **Consequences** | *"Repeated deliberate breaches = professional misconduct"* |
| **Support offer** | *"Review the manual, retraining session, and team meeting review"* |
| **Close** | *"Do you have any questions for me?"* |

***

> 💡 **Exam Tip:** The **critical error** in this station is failing to tell the associate the **correct step-by-step protocol** (stop → remove → hand hygiene → new gloves → resume). Even if everything else is perfect, skipping this makes it a fail. Always state the steps explicitly.

Sources

title: Smoking cessation
content:
Here is the lecture content fully reorganized into a clean, exam-ready study guide for the NDECC SJ (Situational Judgement) exam.

***

# NDECC SJ: Smoking Cessation – Study Guide

## Recognizing the Station Type

In an SJ station, a prompt may seem to overlap two domains (e.g., health promotion **and** a medical questionnaire). Don't get confused — focus on what action is required. In this case:[1]
- The **desire to quit smoking** = Health Promotion task
- The **medical questionnaire** = History-taking duty

Both must be addressed, but the **primary driver** is smoking cessation counselling.[2]

***

## Step 1: Opening & Identify Smoking Status

Begin with a brief, empathetic opening (introduce yourself, confirm patient's desire to quit), then ask targeted questions:[3]

- How long have you been smoking? *(e.g., 15 years)*
- How many cigarettes per day? *(e.g., 14–15 cpd)*
- What type of tobacco? *(e.g., cigarettes)*
- Have you ever tried to quit before? *(e.g., tried chewing gum — no nicotine, so ineffective)*

***

## Step 2: Stages of Change — Assessing Readiness

This determines how deeply you discuss NRT:[4]

| Stage | Patient Response | Your Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Contemplation** | Wants to quit within 6 months, but not 1 month | Supportive listening; avoid overwhelming with NRT details |
| **Preparation** | Wants to quit within the **next 1 month** | ✅ Begin full NRT discussion |
| **Ready (Action)** | Wants to quit immediately / right away | Full NRT counselling + set quit date |

> **Key rule:** Only start detailed NRT counselling when the patient is in the **preparation or action phase** (within 1 month).[4]

***

## Step 3: Medical History — Mandatory Duty

Even when the focus is health promotion, the medical questionnaire must be reviewed. For **this case**, flag and follow up on:[5]

1. **Allergies** — Patient marked "yes" but listed no specific allergen → ask what they are allergic to
2. **Asthma** — Diagnosed a few years ago, reports it is getting worse → ask about severity, current management
3. **Breathing problems** — Also marked "yes" (patient treats it as separate from asthma) → clarify if related

**Clinical link to smoking:** Reinforce that quitting will **not cure asthma**, but continuing to smoke will very likely **worsen** both asthma and breathing difficulties. Use this as a motivator.[6]

***

## Step 4: NRT Counselling (3-Part Approach)

### A. Assess Past Attempts & Lessons Learned
- Ask: *"Have you tried anything to quit before?"*
- Patient tried chewing gum → it failed because regular gum has **no nicotine** — body still experiences full withdrawal[7]

### B. Identify Barriers to Quitting
Common barriers to explore:[3]
- Social smoking (e.g., group at work during breaks)
- Stress-driven smoking
- Ingrained habit (e.g., 15+ years — this patient's case)

### C. Provide NRT Information & Resources
The **ODA/CDA recommends** dental clinics stock NRT materials and the entire team should be educated about cessation options.[4]

***

## NRT Products (OTC — Canada)

| Product | How It Works | Tapering Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Nicotine Patch** | Sustained-release nicotine absorbed through skin; worn on arm/torso | Start: change every 2 days → gradually extend intervals between changes |
| **Nicotine Gum (Lozenge)** | Use whenever urge to smoke arises; replaces each smoking urge | Start at ~15 pieces/day → reduce over weeks/months; **use 4 mg if >25 cpd, 2 mg if <25 cpd** [5] |

**Key rationale for NRT:** After 15 years of smoking, the body cannot tolerate a sudden crash in nicotine. NRT tapers the body off gradually, **reducing withdrawal symptoms** and improving quit compliance.[7]

***

## Step 5: Build the Action Plan

1. **Set a quit date** — involve the patient; a reasonable target is ~4–6 months
2. **Choose NRT product** together (patch vs. gum based on preference)
3. **Schedule follow-up** — e.g., review in 1 week
4. **Offer booklets/resources** on NRT (clinic should have these available)[4]
5. **Reinforce motivation** — link quitting to better control of asthma & breathing

***

## Scope of Practice Boundary

As a **dentist**, your role is: **Identification → Education → Support**.[4]

- ✅ OTC NRT: patches, gum, lozenges — you can recommend these
- ❌ Prescription-based medications (e.g., Varenicline/Champix, Bupropion) — **requires a CE/pharmacotherapy course OR refer to physician**[4]

***

## Quick Exam Checklist

- [ ] Open station: introduce + confirm reason for visit
- [ ] Smoking history: duration, cpd, tobacco type, prior quit attempts
- [ ] Assess readiness to quit: contemplation vs. preparation/action
- [ ] Address medical questionnaire: allergies, asthma, breathing problems
- [ ] Link smoking → worsening asthma (motivational hook)
- [ ] Explain NRT rationale (why gum alone failed)
- [ ] Present NRT options: patch + nicotine gum/lozenge
- [ ] Set action plan with quit date + follow-up
- [ ] Stay within scope; refer to physician if prescription meds needed

Sources
[1] Situational Judgement Preparation Course For NDECC https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-for-ndecc
[2] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[3] Findings and Future Directions from a Smoking Cessation ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11581356/
[4] A Practical Guide to Tobacco Cessation in Dental Offices https://www.cda-adc.ca/jcda/vol-67/issue-3/153.pdf
[5] Dispensing of Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT) https://www.youcanmakeithappen.ca/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/DirectiveDispensingNRTNov2011Final.pdf
[6] Smoking cessation https://www.dentaljuce.com/smoking-cessation-quitting-smoking
[7] Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT) Guidance for Patients https://www.pharmacists.ca/cpha-ca/assets/File/smoking-cessation/Nicotine-Replacement-Therapy-NRT-Guidance-for-Patients_EN2.pdf
[8] NDECC​® - The National Dental Examining Board of ... https://ndeb-bned.ca/equivalency-process/ndecc-2/
[9] Evidence summary: is smoking cessation an effective and cost ... https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21350531/
[10] [PDF] TCFHT-MD10_Nicotine Replacement Therapy - MEDICAL DIRECTIVE https://taddlecreekfht.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/TCFHT-MD10_Nicotine-Replacement-Therapy-2024-06-14-FINAL.pdf
[11] Home https://jcnde.ada.org
[12] NDECC® Situational Judgement Full Course - Prep Doctors https://ca.prepdoctors.com/courses/ndeb-dental-equivalency/ndecc-situational-judgement
[13] NDECC Situational Judgement Exam course https://teachodontist.thinkific.com/courses/NDECC-SJ
[14] Microsoft Word - TCFHT-MD10_Nicotine Replacement Therapy.docx https://taddlecreekfht.ca/wp-content/uploads/2018/07/TCFHT-MD10_Nicotine-Replacement-Therapy.pdf
[15] Exam Resources https://ndeb-bned.ca/resources/exam-resources/
Here is a full, detailed dentist–patient dialogue script structured around the **5 A's framework** — the gold standard for tobacco cessation in dental practice. Use this for active recall and mock practice.[1][2]

***

# NDECC SJ Script: Smoking Cessation Station

## Station Context
> **Scenario:** A patient has filled in a medical questionnaire at your dental clinic. They have indicated a desire to quit smoking. They also marked **yes** to: currently under physician care, taking medications (unlisted), allergies (unlisted), asthma, and breathing problems. They marked **yes** to tobacco use.

***

## Opening — Establish Rapport

> **Dentist:** "Good morning! My name is Dr. [Name], and I'll be your dentist today. Before we get started, I just want to take a few minutes to go over what you've filled in on your questionnaire and have a chat with you. Is that okay?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, of course."

> **Dentist:** "Wonderful. I noticed you mentioned that you'd like to quit smoking — first of all, I really want to commend you for that. That takes courage and it's a really positive step for your overall health. I'd love to support you in that journey today."

***

## The 5 A's in Action

### A1 — ASK: Establish Smoking History

> **Dentist:** "So I can better understand where you're at, could you tell me a little more about your smoking history? How long have you been smoking?"

> **Patient:** "About 14 to 15 years now."

> **Dentist:** "Thank you for sharing that. And roughly how many cigarettes would you say you smoke in a day?"

> **Patient:** "Around 14 to 15 cigarettes a day."

> **Dentist:** "Got it. And I can see on the questionnaire you marked tobacco use — just to be specific, is it cigarettes, or are you using any other form of tobacco such as a pipe or chewing tobacco?"

> **Patient:** "Just cigarettes."

> **Dentist:** "Perfect, thank you. That really helps me understand your situation better."

***

### A2 — ADVISE: Personalized, Clear Message

> **Dentist:** "I do want to take a moment to share with you — and I'll touch on this more in a little bit — that smoking, especially over a long period like yours, can have quite a significant impact on your overall and oral health. The great news is that making a change, even at this stage, can make a real difference. So I'm genuinely glad you've decided to take this step."

***

### A3 — ASSESS: Readiness to Quit (Stages of Change)

> **Dentist:** "Now, I'd love to know a little more about where you are mentally with quitting. Have you ever tried to quit smoking before?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, actually. I tried chewing gum for a while, but it just didn't work. After every piece of gum I still had this huge urge to smoke."

> **Dentist:** "That's a really common experience, and it makes a lot of sense — we'll come back to why that happened in just a moment. Now, in terms of timing — when are you thinking about quitting? Is this something you're considering within the next six months?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, definitely within six months."

> **Dentist:** "That's great. And would you say you're looking at doing this within the next month, or even sooner?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, I want to quit as soon as possible — ideally within the next month."

> **Dentist:** "Excellent. That tells me you are absolutely ready to have this conversation, and I'm very happy to help you put together a real plan today."

> *(Internal note: Patient is in the **Preparation Phase** → proceed with full NRT discussion)*[3]

***

## Medical History Review (Questionnaire Duty)

> **Dentist:** "Before we dive deeper into the cessation plan, I do want to go through a few things on your questionnaire, just to make sure I have a complete picture of your health. You mentioned you're currently under a physician's care — could you tell me what you're seeing your physician for?"

> **Patient:** "Just general health checkups."

> **Dentist:** "Wonderful. And you did mention you're taking some medications — could you let me know what those are? Even vitamins or over-the-counter things count."

> **Patient:** "[Patient lists medications]."

> **Dentist:** "Thank you. Now, you also indicated that you have some allergies — could you tell me specifically what you're allergic to?"

> **Patient:** "I think it's a reaction to a certain shampoo or a face wash. Nothing serious."

> **Dentist:** "Good to know, thank you. That helps me make sure we use the right products here at the clinic."

> **Dentist:** "I also noticed you marked yes for both asthma and breathing problems. Could you tell me a little more about your asthma — when were you diagnosed, and how have things been going?"

> **Patient:** "I was diagnosed a few years ago. Honestly, it feels like it's been getting worse lately."

> **Dentist:** "I'm sorry to hear that. And the breathing problems you listed — are those connected to your asthma, or do you feel they're separate issues?"

> **Patient:** "I think they might be related, but they feel different to me sometimes."

> **Dentist:** "That's a very important observation, and I really appreciate you flagging both. I want to make a connection here — while quitting smoking won't make your asthma disappear overnight, continuing to smoke significantly increases the risk of your asthma and breathing difficulties getting worse over time. So your decision to quit is actually one of the best things you can do for your lungs right now."[3]

***

### A4 — ASSIST: NRT Education & Barrier Identification

> **Dentist:** "Now, let's circle back to your previous quit attempt. You mentioned you tried chewing gum — regular gum, correct?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, just regular gum."

> **Dentist:** "That explains why it didn't work. Regular chewing gum doesn't contain nicotine. After 15 years of smoking, your body has become physically dependent on nicotine. When you stop suddenly, you experience strong withdrawal symptoms — cravings, irritability, anxiety — which is what pushes people back to smoking. What we want to do is taper your body off nicotine gradually, so those withdrawal symptoms are manageable."[4]

> **Dentist:** "This approach is called **Nicotine Replacement Therapy, or NRT**. Have you heard of it before?"

> **Patient:** "I've heard the term but I'm not sure exactly how it works."

> **Dentist:** "Let me explain the two main options available over the counter here in Canada. The first is the **nicotine patch**. You apply it directly to your skin — on your arm or torso — and it releases nicotine slowly and steadily throughout the day. You wear it for about two to three days before replacing it, and over time, we gradually increase the gap between patch changes so your body slowly gets used to less and less nicotine."[4]

> **Dentist:** "The second option is **nicotine gum or lozenges**. Unlike the regular gum you tried, these actually contain nicotine. The idea is that every time you feel the urge to smoke, you use a piece of nicotine gum instead. If you're currently at about 15 cigarettes a day, we'd start you on the 2 mg strength. Over the weeks, we gradually reduce the number of pieces you use each day — maybe starting at 15, then reducing to 12, then 10, and so on — until your body no longer depends on nicotine."[5][4]

> **Dentist:** "Now, I'd also like to ask — do you feel there are any specific situations or triggers that make it hard for you to stop? For example, some patients find they smoke more in social groups at work, or when they're stressed — does anything like that apply to you?"

> **Patient:** "Not really. It's just been so many years — it's become a habit more than anything else."

> **Dentist:** "That's very understandable. Long-term habits can be really difficult to break, but the good news is it absolutely can be done with the right support and plan."

> **Dentist:** "I also have some printed materials here about NRT that I'd love to give you to take home. Our whole team is familiar with this information and can answer any questions you have along the way."[6]

***

### A5 — ARRANGE: Action Plan & Follow-Up

> **Dentist:** "So let's put together a plan together. I'd like to involve you in setting a timeline that feels realistic and achievable. Do you think you'd be comfortable aiming to be completely free of smoking within the next four to six months?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, that sounds reasonable."

> **Dentist:** "Perfect. Let's set that as our target date. Here's what I'd suggest: start with the nicotine patch — put one on tomorrow morning. Come back and see me in one week so we can check in on how you're feeling, whether you're experiencing any withdrawal symptoms, and adjust the plan if needed."[3]

> **Dentist:** "As we move forward, we'll gradually reduce the nicotine dose — stepping down the patch strength every few weeks — until you no longer need it. I'll also keep tracking how your asthma and breathing are doing, because we should start to see some improvement there as well."

> **Dentist:** "And one last thing — if at some point the OTC NRT alone isn't enough, there are prescription-based medications that a physician can prescribe. I'd be happy to refer you to your doctor at that stage if needed, and we'll work together as a team."[7]

> **Dentist:** "Does all of this sound good to you? Do you have any questions at all?"

> **Patient:** "No, this is very helpful. Thank you so much."

> **Dentist:** "You're very welcome. I'm really proud of you for taking this step, and I'll be right here supporting you every step of the way."

***

## Script at a Glance

| Step | Key Action | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **ASK** | Duration, cpd, tobacco type, prior attempts | Always quantify smoking history |
| **ADVISE** | Clear, personalized statement about risk | Keep it empathetic, not judgmental |
| **ASSESS** | Readiness: within 6 months? Within 1 month? | Only proceed to NRT if within 1 month [8] |
| **Medical Hx** | Allergies, asthma, breathing problems | Link asthma worsening to smoking [3] |
| **ASSIST** | Explain why gum failed; introduce patch + NRT gum | Explain tapering mechanism [4] |
| **ARRANGE** | Set quit date, follow-up in 1 week, offer booklet | Refer to physician if OTC NRT insufficient [7] |

Sources
[1] Interventions for Smoking Cessation and Treatments ... - NCBI https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555596/
[2] [PDF] Counseling Your Patients about Tobacco Cessation: The 5A's https://www.sfhp.org/wp-content/files/Counseling-Your-Patients-about-Tobacco-Cessation.pdf
[3] TOBACCO CESSATION GUIDANCE https://www.fdiworlddental.org/sites/default/files/2021-07/FDI%20Tobacco%20Cessation%20Guide%20160621.pdf
[4] Tobacco Use Cessation Services and the Role of the ... https://www.cdha.ca/pdfs/profession/resources/1004_tobacco.pdf
[5] Dispensing of Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT) https://www.youcanmakeithappen.ca/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/DirectiveDispensingNRTNov2011Final.pdf
[6] A Practical Guide to Tobacco Cessation in Dental Offices https://www.cda-adc.ca/jcda/vol-67/issue-3/153.pdf
[7] Situational Judgement Preparation Course For NDECC https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-for-ndecc
[8] Five Major Steps to Intervention (The "5 A's") https://www.ahrq.gov/prevention/guidelines/tobacco/5steps.html
[9] ® https://ndeb-bned.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/NDECC-Protocol_Effective-September-30-2024.pdf
[10] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[11] NDECC​® - The National Dental Examining Board of ... https://ndeb-bned.ca/equivalency-process/ndecc-2/
[12] Situational Judgement - Foundations For NDECC Exam https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-cases-scenarios-for-ndecc-exam
[13] NDECC SITUATIONAL JUDGEMENT EXAM - Full course and Mock exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AnYi3dsQJU0
[14] FREE First Lecture | NDECC Situational Judgement Course | NDEB Equivalency https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1B7Xwpkn2KQ
[15] Course | Situational Judgment https://passingplandental.ca/Courses/CourseDetail/3
[16] NDECC® Situational Judgement Full Course - Prep Doctors https://ca.prepdoctors.com/courses/ndeb-dental-equivalency/ndecc-situational-judgement
[17] NDECC® Situational Judgement Fundamentals https://ca.prepdoctors.com/courses/ndeb-dental-equivalency/ndecc-situational-judgement-fundamentals

title: Incomplete rct
content:
Got it — you want a **professionally organized version** of this long scenario for **NDECC/SJ-style dental exam revision**, focusing on communication, professionalism, and clinical reasoning. Below is a **clear, structured version** of your scenario and approach that you can study and practice.

***

## Scenario Summary

**Situation:**  
A patient whom you haven’t seen in **3 years** presents to your clinic. Two months ago, they had tried to contact your clinic but it was closed for the holidays. They then went to an **emergency clinic** for pain on a tooth, where they were told **root canal treatment (RCT)** was done. They now return asking for a **crown** on that same tooth.

**Findings:**  
A **radiograph** taken today shows an **incomplete root canal treatment**.

**Task:**  
You must **discuss your findings** with the patient in a **professional, objective, and empathetic way**—without criticizing or speculating about the other dentist’s work.

***

## Key Professionalism Focus

This station evaluates your ability to:
- Demonstrate **professional communication** with patients.
- Handle **discrepancies between patient expectations and clinical findings** tactfully.
- Remain **objective, respectful, and patient-centered**.
- Manage **ethical boundaries** — avoid making negative or speculative remarks about another clinician.

***

## Step-by-Step Approach

### 1. Opening and Rapport-Building
- Greet the patient warmly and confirm their understanding of today’s appointment.

> “Hello, it’s nice to see you again after such a long time. I understand you’d like to get a crown for your tooth today.”

- Confirm the patient’s version of events.

> “Could you tell me a bit more about what was done previously on that tooth?”

Patient responds: they had pain, went to an emergency dentist, got a root canal done, and were told to follow up for a crown.

***

### 2. Presenting Your Findings Objectively
Acknowledge their history, then share your findings **without judgment** and **without labeling** previous work as incomplete or poor.

> “Thank you for sharing that. Based on the x-ray I took today, it looks like your tooth still requires some additional treatment before we can proceed with the crown.”

Avoid saying “the root canal was done incorrectly” or “it’s incomplete.”  
Use neutral language such as:
- “needs further work”
- “requires completion”
- “still needs some more cleaning or filling”

***

### 3. Explain the Clinical Implication
Clarify why the crown cannot be placed immediately and what must be done next.

> “Before a crown can be placed, the inside of the tooth needs to be fully sealed to prevent reinfection. From what I see on your x-ray, we’ll need to complete that step first to ensure your tooth is healthy and strong for the crown.”

If straightforward, you may complete the RCT yourself.  
If complicated (e.g., open canal >2 months, possible perforation, etc.), explain need for a specialist:

> “Given how long the tooth has been open, it would be best for a specialist (endodontist) to complete the treatment first, and I can then place the crown.”

***

### 4. Handling Patient Questions Professionally
If the patient expresses confusion or frustration:

> “That’s a good question, and I completely understand your concern. Since I wasn’t present during your previous appointment, I can’t comment on what was discussed or done at that time. I can only share what I’m seeing today and guide you on the next steps.”

Redirect gently if patient insists:

> “If you have any questions about the previous procedure, I’d encourage you to contact that clinic—they’ll be able to clarify what was done. For now, I’ll focus on ensuring your tooth gets the complete treatment it needs.”

***

### 5. Outline the Next Steps
Summarize clearly what will happen next:
- Complete/redo the RCT, or refer to a specialist.
- Once the RCT is finished, proceed with the crown.

> “So to move forward safely, we’ll first complete the treatment inside the tooth. Once that’s done, we’ll take another x-ray to confirm it’s sealed properly and then plan for your crown.”

***

### 6. Maintain Professional Tone Throughout

**Be empathetic and calm:**  
- Avoid blaming or speculation.
- Avoid judging the patient for missed appointments.
- Avoid judging the previous dentist’s decisions.

**Correct Response Style:**
> “Based on the image from today…”  
> “The tooth appears to need a bit more treatment…”  
> “I was not present for that procedure, so I can’t comment…”  
> “Here’s what we can do from this point forward…”

**Avoid Critical Errors:**
- Criticizing the other dentist (“They didn’t do it properly.”)
- Agreeing blindly with the patient and proceeding to crown.
- Showing frustration or sarcasm.
- Making assumptions about negligence.

***

## Key Phrases for Simulation

**Acknowledging the patient:**
> “Thank you for updating me on what happened earlier.”

**Objectively presenting findings:**
> “From the image I’ve taken today, I can see that this tooth still needs additional internal treatment.”

**Providing rationale:**
> “To ensure long-term success and prevent reinfection, this step needs to be completed before placing a crown.”

**Deflecting inappropriate comparison:**
> “As I wasn’t present then, I’m unable to comment on the previous treatment decisions.”

**Redirecting focus:**
> “What’s important now is getting this tooth ready for the crown safely and effectively.”

***

## Summary Table for Quick Revision

| Step | What to Do | Example Phrase |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| 1. Greeting & history | Build rapport, gather background | “I understand you’re here for a crown. Could you tell me what was done before?” |
| 2. Present findings | Stay objective and neutral | “On today’s x-ray, it looks like this tooth still needs a bit more work.” |
| 3. Explain significance | Link to patient goal | “We need to finish the treatment before placing the crown to protect the tooth.” |
| 4. Handle concerns | Stay professional, don’t speculate | “Since I wasn’t present then, I can’t comment, but I can explain what we need to do now.” |
| 5. Plan next steps | Outline treatment or referral | “We’ll complete the root treatment, then proceed with the crown.” |
| 6. Maintain professionalism | Kind, nonjudgmental tone | “I appreciate your patience as we get this done properly.” |

***

Would you like me to write a **sample dialogue** (patient-dentist conversation) following this structured approach for your mock exam 


Below is a **ready-to-practice sample dialogue** you can use for NDECC SJ-style preparation. It follows the scenario you described and emphasizes professionalism, neutrality, and clear communication.  

***

## Sample Dialogue (Dentist–Patient)

**Dentist enters, greets, builds rapport**

**Dentist:**  
Hi, Mr. Smith, it’s nice to see you again after a long time. I understand you’re here today to get a crown on this tooth, is that right?[1]

**Patient:**  
Yes, this tooth was hurting a couple of months ago. I went to a walk-in clinic and they did a root canal. They told me I need a crown, so I came back here to get it done.

**Dentist (acknowledge and invite story):**  
Thank you for explaining that to me. Just so I’m clear, you had pain, went to the walk‑in clinic, they treated the tooth and told you a crown was needed, and now you’re here to have that crown done. Is that correct?[1]

**Patient:**  
Yes, exactly.

***

### Presenting your findings objectively

**Dentist:**  
I appreciate you sharing that. I took an x‑ray of your tooth today, and I’d like to show you what I see.[2][3]

**Patient:**  
Okay.

**Dentist (neutral, non‑critical wording):**  
If you look here at this area on the x‑ray, you can see the inside of the root where the treatment is done. From what I see, your tooth still needs some additional work inside before we can safely place a crown.[3][2]

**Patient:**  
What do you mean? They told me the root canal was done.

**Dentist (objective, not blaming):**  
I understand that’s what you were told, and I can see why this is confusing. What I can tell you is that, based on the x‑ray I took today, the tooth is not yet fully sealed inside. That means we need to do a bit more treatment to clean and fill the root completely, so the tooth is well protected before we put a crown on it.[2][3]

***

### Explaining why more work is needed

**Patient:**  
So you can’t just do the crown today?

**Dentist:**  
To give you the best long‑term result, it’s important that the inside of the tooth is fully treated first. If we put a crown on a tooth that hasn’t been completely treated, there is a higher risk of infection or problems later on. Finishing the treatment inside first helps us protect your tooth and your investment in the crown.[4][3][2]

***

### When the patient questions the previous dentist

**Patient (testing you):**  
Why didn’t the other dentist tell me this? Did they do something wrong?

**Dentist (acknowledge feeling, stay neutral):**  
That’s a very understandable question, and I can see why you’re concerned.[3][4]
Since I wasn’t there during that appointment, I’m not able to comment on what was discussed or what decisions were made at that time. Different dentists may have different approaches or may have planned to see you again to continue the treatment. What I can do is explain clearly what I’m seeing today and what we need to do from this point forward.[5][6][7]

**Patient:**  
So you’re not saying they messed up?

**Dentist:**  
I’m not in a position to judge another dentist’s treatment because I don’t know all the details of that visit. My responsibility is to give you an honest picture of your current situation and guide you on the safest next steps. If you have questions about what was done there, you absolutely have the right to contact that clinic and ask for clarification.[6][7][5]

***

### Offering a clear plan

**Patient:**  
Okay… so what happens now?

**Dentist (offer options and plan):**  
Based on what I see now, there are two main options.[1][3]

1. I can complete the treatment inside the tooth here—finish the cleaning and filling of the root—and once that’s done and looks good on the x‑ray, we go ahead with the crown.  
2. If the tooth is more complex than it appears, or if you prefer, I can refer you to an endodontist, a root‑canal specialist, to complete the treatment first. After they finish, you return here and we place the crown.[8][2][3]

In either case, the first step is to complete the internal treatment, and the second step is the crown you came in for.[3][1]

**Patient:**  
Which option do you recommend?

**Dentist:**  
From what I see right now, I believe we can likely manage this here and complete the treatment for you. If, once I start, I find anything more complicated, I will discuss it with you and we can decide together if a specialist referral is better. My goal is to make sure the tooth is treated thoroughly and that your crown lasts as long as possible.[4][2][3]

***

### Checking understanding and consent

**Dentist:**  
How does that sound to you? Do you have any questions or concerns about what I’ve explained so far?[1][3]

**Patient:**  
No, that makes sense. I just wish I’d known earlier.

**Dentist (empathy and reassurance):**  
I understand it’s frustrating to hear there’s more work needed when you thought it was finished. The good news is that we’ve caught it now, and we can take the proper steps to get this tooth healthy and ready for a crown. We’ll go at a pace you’re comfortable with, and I’ll explain each step as we go.[2][4][3]

**Patient:**  
Okay, let’s go ahead and do what’s needed.

**Dentist:**  
Great. We’ll start by scheduling the appointment to complete the treatment inside the tooth, and then we’ll plan for your crown afterwards. If any questions come up later, please feel free to ask me at any time.[3][1]

***

If you’d like, I can also create a **short bullet-point script** you can memorize (opening, findings, handling criticism, closing) for rapid recall during the exam.

Sources
[1] Beyond The Basics: A Deep Dive Into NDECC's Situational Judgement https://www.wisesouls.ca/post/judgement-day-a-deep-dive-into-ndecc-s-situational-judgement
[2] Patient Communication in Root Canal Therapy https://learn.globalsurgical.com/blog/patient-communication-in-root-canal-therapy
[3] An Overview of Dentist–Patient Communication in Quality Dental ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11763373/
[4] 5 Root Canal Treatment Insights for Better Patient Dentist ... https://dentalcarefree.com/blogs/5-Root-Canal-Treatment-Insights-for-Better-Patient-Dentist-Communication.html
[5] Help! A New Patient Is Complaining About Their Previous Dentist https://www.profsolutions.com/industries/dentists/insurance/risk-management/help-a-new-patient-is-complaining-about-their-previous-dentist/
[6] One of the most difficult issues you will encounter in your daily practice is addressing a patient’s complaint https://dentistcare.com/globalassets/optimized-pdfs/bulletins/justifiable-criticism.pdf
[7] How to Deal with Another Dentist's Mistake - The Dawson Academy https://thedawsonacademy.com/how-to-deal-with-another-dentists-mistake/
[8] How to Use Root Canal Success Strategies for Enhanced Communication - Dentalcarefree https://dentalcarefree.com/blogs/How-to-Use-Root-Canal-Success-Strategies-for-Enhanced-Communication.html
[9] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[10] Situational Judgement Test Practice Paper https://www.copdend.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/DFT-Situational-Judgement-Test-Scenarios.pdf

title: Community grant for snacks
content:
Here’s a cleaned-up, exam-style outline you can memorize and adapt in the ND(E)CC station.

***

## 1. Opening and Information Gathering

1. Greet and appreciate  
   - “Thank you for coming, this is **wonderful** for the community.”  
   - Link oral health to general health and your role as dental professional/health promoter.

2. Clarify target audience  
   - “Who exactly are you targeting: young children, adolescents, adults, geriatrics, or a mix?”  
   - “Are there specific groups: school children, sports clubs, long‑term care residents, pregnant women, etc.?”

3. Clarify logistics and constraints  
   - “Is this a one‑time grant or ongoing funding?”  
   - “Do you already have:  
     - Any contracts with snack suppliers/school canteens/vending machines?  
     - Volunteers or staff to help run activities?”  
   - “Do you have any existing channels to reach people: schools, senior centers, sports clubs, social media?”

4. Clarify communication tools  
   - “How are you planning to get information out: posters, flyers, workshops, school talks, social media, health fairs, snack tasting booths?”  
   - If they say “I don’t know yet”, acknowledge and say you’ll suggest a structured plan and examples.

Then say: “I’ll first give you a general plan that works for most populations, then we can tailor for your specific groups.”

***

## 2. Core Health Messages (General Principles)

Explain in simple language:

- Frequent sugars and refined carbs → more acid attacks → higher caries risk.[1][2][3][4]
- Tooth‑friendly foods are:  
  - Low in added sugar, not sticky, not slowly dissolving in the mouth.[5]
  - Higher in fiber or protein and require chewing (stimulate saliva).[6][7]
- Best beverages for teeth are plain water and plain milk; sugary and acidic drinks are harmful.[7][8]

Use one simple example:  
- “Sipping juice or pop all afternoon is much worse than having a small dessert once with a meal.”

***

## 3. Tooth-Friendly Snacks: What to Recommend

Emphasize: crunchy, low sugar, non‑sticky, saliva‑stimulating, nutrient‑dense.[5][6][7]

### A. Recommended snacks

1. Fresh fruits (whole, not juice)  
   - Apples, pears, berries, watermelon, etc. (emphasize whole fruit, not dried or juiced).[6][7]
   - High water and fiber content help “self‑clean” teeth.

2. Crunchy vegetables  
   - Carrots, celery, cucumbers, bell peppers, cherry tomatoes.[7][6]
   - “Natural toothbrush effect”: they help disrupt plaque and stimulate saliva.

3. Dairy products (unsweetened)  
   - Cheese cubes/sticks, plain yogurt, plain kefir.[9][1][6][7]
   - Provide calcium and phosphorus, help neutralize acid, form a protective environment for enamel.

4. Protein‑rich snacks  
   - Nuts and seeds (if allergy‑safe), boiled eggs, hummus with veggie sticks, tofu pieces, beans/lentil snacks.[9][5][6]
   - Filling, reduce frequent sugar snacking.

5. Whole‑grain, low‑sugar options  
   - Whole‑grain crackers or toast with cheese, small whole‑grain sandwiches with lean protein, low‑sugar homemade muffins, oats.[1][5][9]

6. Popcorn (special case)  
   - Plain, air‑popped, unflavoured popcorn as a better movie snack than candy and caramel popcorn.[9]
   - Warn about kernels getting stuck and the need for good brushing.

### B. Snacks to limit or avoid

Explain both caries risk and stickiness/frequency.[2][4][5]

- Sticky, long‑lasting sweets: toffees, caramels, fruit roll‑ups, gummies, sticky granola bars.[4][5][7][9]
- Highly refined starchy snacks that stick: chips, crackers, pretzels, nachos, sweet muffins.[5][7][9]
- Sweetened yogurts and flavored dairy: “fruit‑on‑the‑bottom”, drinking yogurts with high sugar.[4][1]
- Dried fruits and fruit leathers (very concentrated sugar and sticky).[4][5][9]

Reinforce:  
- “If they do have these, it’s safer with a main meal instead of as frequent between‑meal snacks.”[2][5]

***

## 4. Tooth-Friendly Beverages: What to Recommend

Make sure you cover beverages clearly, since that was a common omission.

### A. Recommended beverages

1. Plain water  
   - First choice at all ages; fluoridated tap water if available.[8][7]
   - Between meals and snacks, water should be the only routine drink.

2. Plain milk (depending on audience)  
   - Especially for children and some older adults needing calcium/protein.[8][1][7][9]
   - Emphasize: not chocolate, not flavored, no added sugar or syrups.

3. Unsweetened homemade smoothies (occasional, with rules)  
   - Made at home with whole fruits/vegetables, no added sugar/honey/syrups.  
   - Advise to have them with meals, not sipped over long periods.

### B. Beverages to limit or avoid

Highlight both sugar and acidity.[3][2][7][8][4]

- Soft drinks/pop, energy drinks, and sports drinks.  
- Fruit juices (even “100% juice”) and fruit punches.  
- Sweetened iced teas, flavored waters, vitamin waters with sugar.  
- Flavored milks, milkshakes, hot chocolate mixes with sugar.  
- Sugary coffees and bubble teas.

Key phrasing:  
- “Even 100% fruit juice is still concentrated sugar. It is much better to eat the fruit and drink water.”[3][1][2][4]

Also stress frequency:  
- “Sipping sugary or acidic drinks over hours is especially damaging, because teeth are under constant acid attack.”[2][3][4]

***

## 5. Behaviour and Timing Advice

Short, exam‑friendly points:

- Try to keep sugar‑containing foods and drinks to main mealtimes.[2][5]
